saanvi_emmadi.name: Saanvi Emmadi
saanvi_emmadi.summary:
A Virginia competitor, Saanvi Emmadi is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Saanvi's.

Saanvi competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
saanvi_emmadi.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Saanvi Emmadi file just landed on our desk. Saanvi hails from Virginia, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Saanvi is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15), and so far, the only thing Saanvi has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Saanvi saga began at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Saanvi has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Saanvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U15, which means Saanvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15), where Saanvi entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Saanvi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Saanvi is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Saanvi wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

saanvi_gupta.name: Saanvi Gupta
saanvi_gupta.summary:
Representing Illinois, Saanvi Gupta is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U17 level, with 5 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Saanvi especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
saanvi_gupta.roast:
Step right up and meet Saanvi Gupta, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Saanvi is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Midwest CRC, and so far, the only thing Saanvi has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Saanvi saga began at 2024 Midwest CRC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Saanvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Saanvi has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Saanvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in GS U17, which means Saanvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 Midwest CRC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Saanvi keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Saanvi has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Saanvi knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

saanvi_jha.name: Saanvi Jha
saanvi_jha.summary:
Representing California, Saanvi Jha is relentlessly knocking on the door, currently competing at the U11 level, reaching 3 finals across 12 entries. A player who reaches that many championship matches has all the tools — the breakthrough title is coming.

Saanvi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Saanvi has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Matthew Alexander Lau (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Alison Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Saanvi's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
saanvi_jha.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Saanvi Jha, from California, currently competing at the U11 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Saanvi's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Saanvi keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Saanvi saga began at 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

If Saanvi's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Saanvi has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships, where Saanvi entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U11, XD U11, GD U11). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Saanvi has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Saanvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Saanvi has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Saanvi keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Saanvi has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Saanvi knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Saanvi has a finals record that should come with a trigger warning, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

saanya_sreenivas.name: Saanya Sreenivas
saanya_sreenivas.summary:
Out of California, Saanya Sreenivas broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 26 entries across 10 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Saanya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Saanya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Samyukta Karthik (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Having moved up to U17, Saanya is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Saanya brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Saanya has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, NorCal, Saanya isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Saanya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
saanya_sreenivas.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Saanya Sreenivas, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Saanya has 26 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Saanya saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 OBA NW OLC in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Saanya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 13 entries — a stretch where Saanya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Saanya is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Can we talk about Saanya and Girls' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Saanya can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Saanya still cannot reach it.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Saanya would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Saanya has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Saanya has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Saanya's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Saanya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Saanya is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Saanya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Saanya's texts. The go-to partner is Samyukta Karthik (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Derek Royce Chan has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Saanya's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Saanya has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Saanya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Saanya has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Saanya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Saanya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Saanya entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Saanya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Saanya story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has turned Girls' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. But every season Saanya comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Saanya, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

saatvik_b_sunilraj.name: Saatvik B. Sunilraj
saatvik_b_sunilraj.summary:
A Virginia competitor, Saatvik B. Sunilraj has collected 3 tournament titles, currently competing at the U19 level, capturing 3 titles across 19 tournaments and 53 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Saatvik keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Saatvik has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Saatvik is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Saatvik adapts to anyone:
  • Kevin Zhao — a trusted BD/U17 partner (6 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Anvitha B Ranganna — a trusted U17/XD partner (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Yoning Liu — 3 events in XD, best: #2, across 2 seasons

Saatvik has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Saatvik has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Saatvik consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Saatvik is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
saatvik_b_sunilraj.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Saatvik B. Sunilraj file just landed on our desk. Saatvik hails from Virginia, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 53 career entries in 19 tournaments, Saatvik has 3 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 16 entries where Saatvik drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 53 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Saatvik saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2022 NE CRC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Saatvik's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Saatvik actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Saatvik is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Boys' Singles finals and Saatvik — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Saatvik has turned 2 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Saatvik's trophies to save time.

If Saatvik's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Saatvik has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 South OLC (TBTT), where Saatvik entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U17, XD U17, BD U17). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Saatvik does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Saatvik has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 3 wins means Saatvik is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Saatvik plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Saatvik is 0-for-17. Zero titles in 17 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Saatvik and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Saatvik needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Saatvik has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Saatvik's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Anvitha B Ranganna: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Anvitha B Ranganna has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Yoning Liu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Saatvik's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Saatvik entered 6 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Saatvik's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Saatvik and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Saatvik has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Then there is the seeding situation. Saatvik has been seeded in 8 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Saatvik takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in BS U17, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Saatvik. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Saatvik has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Saatvik is not just losing locally — Saatvik is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sunilraj family travels cross-country so Saatvik can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Saatvik has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Saatvik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U17, which means Saatvik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Saatvik entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Saatvik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Saatvik's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 50 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Saatvik is a player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Singles. Will Saatvik figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Saatvik's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

saatvik_dinesh.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Saatvik Dinesh, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Saatvik is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 NE CRC, and so far, the only thing Saatvik has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Saatvik saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Saatvik has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Saatvik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BS U15, which means Saatvik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), where Saatvik entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Saatvik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Saatvik keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Saatvik has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Saatvik, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

saatvik_shetty.name: Saatvik Shetty
saatvik_shetty.summary:
Representing California, Saatvik Shetty is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-3 finish at Selection places Saatvik in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Saatvik is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Saatvik competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 3 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U17 BD) • Junior Nationals champion (BS U17) • 7 ORC titles.

Saatvik has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Vincent Chen (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (5 total events in BD/U17, across 2 seasons)
  • Annie Meng (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Katie Wong (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Terence Cai (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 4 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Saatvik is still ascending.

Saatvik has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Saatvik has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Saatvik has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 9 titles, Saatvik has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
saatvik_shetty.roast:
Step right up and meet Saatvik Shetty, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. And yes, Saatvik IS a Junior National Champion — BS U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 59-entry career across 26 tournaments with a 15% win rate, which means 50 times — 50! — Saatvik went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 59 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Saatvik saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Saatvik's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 9 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Can we talk about Saatvik and Boys' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 5 finals, 2 losses. Saatvik can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Saatvik still cannot reach it.

Here is a fun detail: in BS U15, Saatvik used to be seeded #1 (at 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals). Tournament committees are literally telling Saatvik 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Saatvik experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Saatvik has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (5 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Saatvik does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Saatvik has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 9 wins means Saatvik is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Saatvik plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 5 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Saatvik can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Saatvik has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Saatvik's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aryan Mohan: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aryan Mohan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Aryan Mohan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Saatvik entered 3 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Saatvik's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Saatvik and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Saatvik has been seeded in 20 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Saatvik takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BS U15, and finishing 2nd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Saatvik. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Saatvik of is playing it safe. Saatvik has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Saatvik has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 9 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Saatvik has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Saatvik is not just losing locally — Saatvik is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shetty family travels cross-country so Saatvik can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Saatvik has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Saatvik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U13, which means Saatvik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NorCal CRC, where Saatvik entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Saatvik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Saatvik's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 9 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 50 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Saatvik is a player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Singles. Will Saatvik figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Saatvik's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sadie_conklin.name: Sadie Conklin
sadie_conklin.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Sadie Conklin is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U17 level, with 5 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Sadie competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Sadie has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
sadie_conklin.roast:

saee_thorat.name: Saee Thorat
saee_thorat.summary:
A California competitor, Saee Thorat has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Saee's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Saee competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (10 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Saee has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 25 different players across the career:
  • Aarav Gupta (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Renee Leung (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U15 (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Jessica Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Celine Ao (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Saee has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Saee has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Saee brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
saee_thorat.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Saee Thorat file just landed on our desk. Saee hails from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 70 career entries in 27 tournaments, Saee has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 69 entries where Saee drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 70 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Saee saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Saee plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 70 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Saee has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Saee's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aarav Gupta: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aarav Gupta has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GD U15 — we hope Renee Leung has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Saee entered 10 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Saee's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Saee and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Saee has shown up 9 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Saee is not just losing locally — Saee is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Thorat family travels cross-country so Saee can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Saee has 37 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Saee showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Saee traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Saee entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Saee paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 70 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Saee. Saee just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Saee has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Saee. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Saee: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

sagasraa_karnan.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Sagasraa Karnan, from Tennessee, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 win across 3 entries in 1 tournaments. That is a 33% conversion rate, which means roughly 7 out of every 10 times Sagasraa shows up to a tournament, Sagasraa pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Sagasraa saga began at 2025 Fortius South CRC, where Sagasraa burst onto the scene with a title in GD U15 - Position 5-8 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Sagasraa has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Sagasraa promised and what Sagasraa has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sagasraa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sagasraa is 0-for-1. Zero titles in 1 tries. The best singles result is a 17th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sagasraa and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sagasraa needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sagasraa has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sagasraa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Fortius South CRC in GS U15, which means Sagasraa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Sagasraa is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Sagasraa wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

sahana_samarthy.name: Sahana Samarthy
sahana_samarthy.summary:
Out of Illinois, Sahana Samarthy is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U15 level, capturing 2 titles across 14 tournaments and 39 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Sahana keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Sahana is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Sahana has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Paige Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Catherine Du (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

Sahana is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 2 titles across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Sahana is still ascending.

Sahana has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Sahana isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 2 titles, Sahana has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
sahana_samarthy.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Sahana Samarthy, from Illinois, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sahana has 39 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sahana saga began at 2023 Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2026 CBA Midwest OLC in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sahana's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 9 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

In Girls' Doubles, Sahana has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sahana chokes in Girls' Doubles finals specifically.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Sahana reaches a final. Sahana has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Sahana does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Sahana has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Sahana is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sahana plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sahana can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Sahana has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sahana's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alex Jiang: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alex Jiang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Alex Jiang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sahana's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Sahana has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Sahana has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sahana has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sahana showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Sahana traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Sahana entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Sahana paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sahana's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 37 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sahana is a player who has turned Girls' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. Will Sahana figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sahana's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sahana_srinivasan.roast:
Allow us to introduce Sahana Srinivasan, from Maryland, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Sahana's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Sahana keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Sahana saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sahana plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Sahana has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U17 — we hope Zeyue Du has emotionally recovered.

Sahana has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Sahana has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sahana has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sahana showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in GS U15, which means Sahana traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 South Frisco ORC, where Sahana entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Sahana paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Sahana keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sahana has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sahana, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Sahasra paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sahasra's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 9 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sahasra is a player who has turned Mixed Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. Will Sahasra figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sahasra's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sahasra_kommineni.name: Sahasra Kommineni
sahasra_kommineni.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Sahasra Kommineni has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Sahasra's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Sahasra especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Sahasra has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Ian Yip (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Evan Liu — 2 events in XD, best: #4

Sahasra has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Sahasra isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Sahasra brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sahasra_kommineni.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Sahasra Kommineni, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sahasra has 16 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sahasra saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sahasra plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Sahasra has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sahasra's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Ian Yip has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Sahasra has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Sahasra has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sahasra has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sahasra showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Sahasra traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Sahasra entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Sahasra paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Sahasra keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sahasra has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sahasra, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

sahasra_reddy.name: Sahasra Reddy
sahasra_reddy.summary:
Representing California, Sahasra Reddy has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, capturing an impressive 3 ORC titles across 2 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Sahasra as one of the premier players on the circuit.

Sahasra is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Sahasra is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 3 ORC titles.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Sahasra adapts to anyone:
  • Mihika Jaiswal (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U15 (10 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Phillip You (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Prathik Nemani (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Sophia Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Sahasra is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Sahasra has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Sahasra has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Sahasra consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 4 titles, Sahasra is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
sahasra_reddy.roast:
Step right up and meet Sahasra Reddy, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 80 career entries in 29 tournaments, Sahasra has 4 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 19 entries where Sahasra drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 80 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sahasra saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sahasra's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 13 entries — a stretch where Sahasra actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sahasra is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In Mixed Doubles, Sahasra has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sahasra chokes in Mixed Doubles finals specifically.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Sahasra reaches a final. Sahasra has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sahasra does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Sahasra has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 4 wins means Sahasra is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sahasra plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sahasra can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Sahasra has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sahasra's texts. The go-to partner is Abhigna Parvathaneni (11 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GD U15 — we hope Mihika Jaiswal has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sahasra entered 7 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sahasra's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sahasra and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Sahasra has shown up 9 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

One thing nobody can accuse Sahasra of is playing it safe. Sahasra has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Sahasra has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Sahasra has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sahasra is not just losing locally — Sahasra is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Reddy family travels cross-country so Sahasra can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sahasra has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sahasra showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Sahasra traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Sahasra entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Sahasra paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Sahasra story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 4 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who the undisputed champion of second place in Mixed Doubles. But every season Sahasra comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Sahasra, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.
