Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sahasra keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sahasra has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sahasra knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Sahasra changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

sahasra_vudum.name: Sahasra Vudum
sahasra_vudum.summary:
Out of Texas, Sahasra Vudum has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U11 level, capturing 2 titles across 6 tournaments and 17 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Sahasra keeps finding ways to come out on top.

A true triple-threat, Sahasra competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Sahasra adapts to anyone:
  • Aaditya Lanka (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Vedha R Chegireddy (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events)
  • Devansh Shambu Ponnapalli (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Sahasra isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.
sahasra_vudum.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Sahasra Vudum, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 2 wins across 17 entries in 6 tournaments. That is a 12% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Sahasra shows up to a tournament, Sahasra pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Sahasra saga began at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 CBA Midwest OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sahasra's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sahasra plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sahasra can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Sahasra has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sahasra's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in XD U11 — we hope Aaditya Lanka has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sahasra's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Sahasra has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sahasra has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sahasra showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in GS U11, which means Sahasra traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip.

Sahasra is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Sahasra wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

sahir_bhuiyan.name: Sahir Bhuiyan
sahir_bhuiyan.summary:
Hailing from Virginia, Sahir Bhuiyan is relentlessly knocking on the door, currently competing at the U13 level, reaching 3 finals across 44 entries. A player who reaches that many championship matches has all the tools — the breakthrough title is coming.

Sahir is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Sahir has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 19 different players across the career:
  • Marcus K Chua (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Luke Wen (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2)
  • Kyle Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 8 tournaments. Sahir is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Sahir has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Sahir has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Sahir brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sahir_bhuiyan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Sahir Bhuiyan, from Virginia, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sahir has 44 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sahir saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U11) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

In Boys' Doubles, Sahir has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sahir chokes in Boys' Doubles finals specifically.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Sahir experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Sahir has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. And remember: Sahir has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sahir plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 44 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Sahir has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sahir's texts. The go-to partner is Marcus K Chua (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U11 — we hope Lukas Ye has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Sahir has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sahir is not just losing locally — Sahir is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Bhuiyan family travels cross-country so Sahir can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sahir has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sahir showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Sahir traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Sahir entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Sahir paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Sahir keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sahir has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sahir, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sai saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sai's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Sai actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sai is 0-for-14. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Boys' Singles finals and Sai — name a more iconic tragedy. 3 trips to the championship match, and Sai has turned 3 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Sai's trophies to save time.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Sai reaches a final. Sai has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 Midwest ORC, where Sai entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sai does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Sai has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 3 wins means Sai is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sai plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sai is 0-for-18. Zero titles in 18 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sai and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sai needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Sai has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sai's texts. The go-to partner is Aditya Namboodiripad (11 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Sathvika Shettipelly has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has seen Sai 5 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sai is not just losing locally — Sai is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shankar family travels cross-country so Sai can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sai has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sai showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11, which means Sai traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Sai entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Sai paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Sai story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 3 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Singles. But every season Sai comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Sai, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

sai_sahasra_gajula.name: Sai Sahasra Gajula
sai_sahasra_gajula.summary:
Representing California, Sai Sahasra Gajula is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Sai in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Sai has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Sai is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U17 GD) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Sai has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 17 different players across the career:
  • Karena Ling (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Aagnaey Gulla Pulijal — a trusted U15/XD partner (17 events, best: #5, spanning 4 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Sai has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Sai has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience, Sai brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sai_sahasra_gajula.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Sai Sahasra Gajula, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 71 career entries in 24 tournaments, Sai has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 70 entries where Sai drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 71 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sai saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 6 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Sai would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Sai has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Sai has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Sai's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sai plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 71 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Sai has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sai's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aagnaey Gulla Pulijal: 17 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 17 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aagnaey Gulla Pulijal has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Diyaa Sarath has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sai entered 12 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Sai's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sai and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Sai has shown up 9 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Sai has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sai is not just losing locally — Sai is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Gajula family travels cross-country so Sai can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sai has 36 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sai showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Sai traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Sai entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Sai paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 6 seasons and 71 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Sai is not most humans. Sai is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Sai the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Sai. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

sajith_kamal.name: Sajith Kamal
sajith_kamal.summary:
A Georgia competitor, Sajith Kamal knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 30 entries across 10 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Sajith competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Sajith has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Tharun Katharnath (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Meghna Yeluguri (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Aradhana Santhosh (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, spanning 3 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Sajith is still ascending.

Sajith has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Sajith is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
sajith_kamal.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Sajith Kamal file just landed on our desk. Sajith hails from Georgia, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 win across 30 entries in 10 tournaments. That is a 3% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Sajith shows up to a tournament, Sajith pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Sajith saga began at 2023 South CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Fortius South CRC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sajith's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Sajith would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Sajith has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Sajith has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Sajith's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sajith plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sajith is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 9th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sajith and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sajith needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Sajith has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Tharun Katharnath (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Aradhana Santhosh has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sajith's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Sajith has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sajith has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sajith showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13, which means Sajith traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Sajith entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Sajith paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sajith's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 29 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sajith is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Sajith figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sajith's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sam_nisenbaum.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Sam Nisenbaum, from Illinois, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Sam is right on schedule. The Sam saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Notably, Samanvi has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Samanvi competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries).

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Samanvi adapts to anyone:
  • Lydia Ng (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (4 events, best: #5)
  • Vivek Rai (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Charity Lam — a trusted GD/U17 partner (5 events, best: #6, across 2 seasons)

Samanvi has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Samanvi has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Samanvi brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
samanvi_boppana.roast:
Allow us to introduce Samanvi Boppana, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 44 career entries in 19 tournaments, Samanvi has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 43 entries where Samanvi drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 44 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Samanvi saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Samanvi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 44 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Samanvi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Samanvi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Charity Lam: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Charity Lam has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Vivek Rai has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Samanvi entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Samanvi's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Samanvi and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Samanvi has shown up 7 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

Samanvi has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Samanvi is not just losing locally — Samanvi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Boppana family travels cross-country so Samanvi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samanvi has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samanvi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17, which means Samanvi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Samanvi entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Samanvi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 5 seasons and 44 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Samanvi is not most humans. Samanvi is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Samanvi the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Samanvi. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

samarth_vikram_urs.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Samarth Vikram Urs file just landed on our desk. Samarth hails from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 19 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 19-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Samarth's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Samarth keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Samarth saga began at 2023 South CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Samarth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 19 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Samarth has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Janelle Zhou (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Janelle Zhou has emotionally recovered.

Samarth has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samarth has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samarth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Samarth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Samarth entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Samarth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Samarth keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Samarth has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Samarth, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Samiksh has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Samiksh has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Samiksh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
samiksh_talapally.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Samiksh Talapally, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 55 career entries in 23 tournaments, Samiksh has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 54 entries where Samiksh drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 55 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Samiksh saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

In Boys' Doubles, Samiksh has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Samiksh chokes in Boys' Doubles finals specifically.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Samiksh experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Samiksh has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC, 2023 MassBad NE OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Samiksh has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Samiksh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 55 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Samiksh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Samiksh's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Neel J Reddy: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Neel J Reddy has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Vrishin Telikuntla has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Samiksh entered 4 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Samiksh's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Samiksh and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Samiksh has shown up 5 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Samiksh is not just losing locally — Samiksh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Talapally family travels cross-country so Samiksh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samiksh has 34 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samiksh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Samiksh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Samiksh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 97th. That is an average placement of 65th, which is a polite way of saying Samiksh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 55 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Samiksh. Samiksh just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Samiksh has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Samiksh. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Samiksh: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

sammi_pan.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Sammi Pan, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-6. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sammi has 5 career entries across 2 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sammi saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 6th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sammi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Sammi has shown up 3 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

Sammi has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sammi has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sammi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17, which means Sammi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Sammi entered 3 events with results ranging from 6th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Sammi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sammi keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sammi has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sammi knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U11) in BS U11, which means Samuel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 NE MassBad OLC, where Samuel entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Samuel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Samuel keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Samuel has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Samuel, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

samuel_jiaoyang_li.name: Samuel Jiaoyang Li
samuel_jiaoyang_li.summary:
Hailing from New Jersey, Samuel Jiaoyang Li is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (BD U19) puts Samuel in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Samuel has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Samuel competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (4 entries) • Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U19) • 2 ORC titles.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Samuel adapts to anyone:
  • Sydney Li (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in U19/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Matthew Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Thomas Wang — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Samuel has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Samuel has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 2 titles, Samuel has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
samuel_jiaoyang_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Samuel Jiaoyang Li, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 24 career entries in 12 tournaments, Samuel has 2 titles — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Samuel drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 24 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Samuel saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 6th. The first title would not come until 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Samuel's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Samuel actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Samuel is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Can we talk about Samuel and Boys' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 3 finals, 2 losses. Samuel can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Samuel still cannot reach it.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Samuel reaches a final. Samuel has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Samuel does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Samuel has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 2 wins means Samuel is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Samuel plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Samuel is 0-for-4. Zero titles in 4 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Samuel and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Samuel needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Samuel has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Samuel's texts. The go-to partner is Sydney Li (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Jennifer Cui has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Samuel entered 4 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Samuel's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Samuel and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Samuel has competed 5 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Samuel has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Samuel is not just losing locally — Samuel is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Samuel can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-25 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samuel has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samuel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 25th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17, which means Samuel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles, 24 entries, and 22 that ended without a trophy. A player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Doubles. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Samuel looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Samuel. We will be watching.

A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U15 — we hope Naresh Sriram has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samuel has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samuel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Samuel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Samuel entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Samuel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

4 seasons. 21 entries. Zero titles. If this were a stock, the SEC would have delisted it by now. If this were a restaurant, the health inspector would have questions. If this were a dating profile, the app would gently suggest 'trying a different approach.' But this is junior badminton, where hope springs eternal and so does Samuel's entry form. We salute the commitment. We question the strategy. We will absolutely be back next season to see if anything changes. (It will not.)

samuel_xiao.roast:

The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Samuel is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 NorCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Samuel has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Samuel saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Samuel is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Samuel's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Samuel is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samuel has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samuel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Samuel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. samyukta_karthik.name: Samyukta Karthik
samyukta_karthik.summary:
Hailing from California, Samyukta Karthik has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 47 entries across 17 tournaments, Samyukta is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

What makes Samyukta especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Samyukta adapts to anyone:
  • Saanya Sreenivas (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Bhavesh Sai Varma Tammu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Samyukta has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Samyukta has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Samyukta brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
samyukta_karthik.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Samyukta Karthik, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Samyukta has 47 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Samyukta saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

If Samyukta's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Samyukta has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 OBA NW OLC, 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Samyukta has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Samyukta plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 47 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Samyukta has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Samyukta's texts. The go-to partner is Dhruvan Karthik (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Agastya Kalal has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Samyukta has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Samyukta is not just losing locally — Samyukta is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Karthik family travels cross-country so Samyukta can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samyukta has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samyukta showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Samyukta traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Samyukta entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Samyukta paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 47 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Samyukta. Samyukta just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Samyukta has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Samyukta. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Samyukta: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Samyuktha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Samyuktha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Surya Peechara (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)

Samyuktha has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Samyuktha has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Samyuktha's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
samyuktha_tadinada.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Samyuktha Tadinada, from North Carolina, continues to exist, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 16 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 16-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Samyuktha's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Samyuktha keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Samyuktha saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

In Mixed Doubles, Samyuktha has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Samyuktha chokes in Mixed Doubles finals specifically.

Here is a fun detail: in GS U11, Samyuktha used to be seeded #1 (at 2023 South CRC). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2023 NE OLC (NVBC)). Tournament committees are literally telling Samyuktha 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Samyuktha experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Samyuktha has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Samyuktha has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Samyuktha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Samyuktha has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Surya Peechara (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in XD U11 — we hope Kayden Hossain has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Samyuktha has been seeded in 5 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Samyuktha takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2023 South OLC in GS U11, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Samyuktha. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Samyuktha has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Samyuktha has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samyuktha has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samyuktha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in XD U11, which means Samyuktha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Season after season, Samyuktha keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Samyuktha has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Samyuktha, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

  • Eesha Vikram (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)

Samyutha has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Samyutha has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

samyutha_aluri.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Samyutha Aluri, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U13 level. We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Samyutha saga began at 2023 South CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Samyutha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 4 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Samyutha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 3 wins in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Samyutha experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Samyutha has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed.
