It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Samyutha does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Samyutha has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 7 wins means Samyutha is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Samyutha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 3 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Samyutha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Samyutha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Samyutha's texts. The go-to partner is Shubhan Sarikonda (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Franklin Xu Cheng has emotionally recovered.

Of Samyutha's 7 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Samyutha feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Junior Nationals has seen Samyutha 3 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Samyutha is not just losing locally — Samyutha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Aluri family travels cross-country so Samyutha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Samyutha has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Samyutha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11, which means Samyutha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Samyutha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Samyutha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 7 titles, 46 entries, and 39 that ended without a trophy. A player who chokes in finals like it is a paid hobby. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the moments of brilliance — Samyutha looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Samyutha. We will be watching.

All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Devnath family travels cross-country so Sanchay can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sanchay has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sanchay showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Sanchay traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Sanchay entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Sanchay paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 13 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Sanchay. Sanchay just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Sanchay has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Sanchay. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Sanchay: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

A true triple-threat, Sanchita competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (1 entry).

Sanchita has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, SoCal, Sanchita isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Sanchita brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sanchita_devnath.roast:

Sanchita has 12 career entries across 5 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sanchita saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 1 entries and a best of 49th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Sanchita plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Sanchita has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' So here we are: 4 seasons, 12 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. sanchita_pandey.name: Sanchita Pandey
sanchita_pandey.summary:
Sanchita Pandey is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (Girl's Singles, XD U17, GD U19). A top-2 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Sanchita in rarified international company.

What makes Sanchita especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior silver medalist (Girl's Singles, XD U17, GD U19, 2017, 2018, 2021).

In doubles, Sanchita has formed effective partnerships:
  • Esther Shi — 2 events in GD, best: #2
  • Dean Tan — 2 events in XD, best: #3

Sanchita has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Sanchita brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sanchita_pandey.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Sanchita Pandey, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Sanchita is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (Girl's Singles, XD U17, GD U19), which means Sanchita has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Sanchita's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Sanchita went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Sanchita represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 5 career entries and a 0% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 5 entries worth of material.

The Sanchita saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Can we talk about Sanchita and Girls' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Sanchita can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Sanchita still cannot reach it.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Sanchita experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Sanchita has reached the finals 2 times — at 2018 Pan Am Junior — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Sanchita has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sanchita plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Season after season, Sanchita keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sanchita has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sanchita, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

sandra_chen.name: Sandra Chen

Sandra has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Aiden Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Sandra has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Sandra brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sandra_chen.roast:
Allow us to introduce Sandra Chen, from New York, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 27 events across 10 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 27-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Sandra's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Sandra keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Sandra saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If Sandra's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Sandra has reached the finals 2 times — at 2024 NE MassBad OLC, 2025 NE MassBad OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Sandra has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sandra plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Sandra has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sandra's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aiden Chen: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aiden Chen has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope James Xu has emotionally recovered.

Sandra has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Sandra has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sandra has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sandra showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Sandra traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Sandra entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Sandra paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 27 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Sandra. Sandra just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Sandra has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Sandra. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Sandra: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Sanjana is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Sanjana wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

sanjana_thouda.name: Sanjana Thouda
sanjana_thouda.summary:
A Texas competitor, Sanjana Thouda is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U17 level, with 7 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Sanjana especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
sanjana_thouda.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Sanjana Thouda file just landed on our desk. Sanjana hails from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 7 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Sanjana is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, and so far, the only thing Sanjana has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Sanjana saga began at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sanjana plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 7 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sanjana has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sanjana showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U17, which means Sanjana traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Sanjana entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Sanjana paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Sanjana keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sanjana has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sanjana, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

sanjanaa_shyam.name: Sanjanaa Shyam
sanjanaa_shyam.summary:
Hailing from North Carolina, Sanjanaa Shyam has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in GD U15. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Sanjanaa belongs with the best in the country.

Sanjanaa is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Sanjanaa is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (GD U15) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Sanjanaa has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 25 different players across the career:
  • Diya Shetty (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)
  • Yalan Zhu (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2024-2025 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Sanjanaa is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Sanjanaa has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Sanjanaa has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Sanjanaa consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Sanjanaa is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
sanjanaa_shyam.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Sanjanaa Shyam, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 70 career entries in 26 tournaments, Sanjanaa has 5 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 13 entries where Sanjanaa drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 70 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sanjanaa saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sanjanaa's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 26 entries — a stretch where Sanjanaa actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sanjanaa is 0-for-14. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In GS U17, Sanjanaa was once THE player to beat — 2 titles including at 2023 South OLC. These days, Sanjanaa is finishing 4th, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date. Remember when Sanjanaa used to win GD U17? Because Sanjanaa sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Sanjanaa took gold at 2024 South CRC (TBTT), and since then? Best finish: 4th. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left.

In Girls' Doubles, Sanjanaa has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sanjanaa chokes in Girls' Doubles finals specifically.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Sanjanaa experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Sanjanaa has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sanjanaa does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Sanjanaa has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 5 wins means Sanjanaa is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sanjanaa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sanjanaa can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Sanjanaa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sanjanaa's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Prabhat Mandava: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Prabhat Mandava has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Prabhat Mandava has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sanjanaa's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sanjanaa entered 6 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Sanjanaa's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sanjanaa and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Sanjanaa 6 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

One thing nobody can accuse Sanjanaa of is playing it safe. Sanjanaa has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Sanjanaa has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Sanjanaa has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sanjanaa is not just losing locally — Sanjanaa is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shyam family travels cross-country so Sanjanaa can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sanjanaa has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sanjanaa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Sanjanaa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Sanjanaa entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Sanjanaa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sanjanaa's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 5 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 65 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sanjanaa is a player who proof that glory has an expiration date and Sanjanaa's is past due. Will Sanjanaa figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sanjanaa's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

Hailing from North Carolina, Sanjit Pravinkumar has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 10 entries in 5 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

What makes Sanjit especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Sanjit has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Vishwajeeth Madhavan (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #3)

Sanjit has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Sanjit's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
sanjit_pravinkumar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Sanjit Pravinkumar, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Sanjit's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Sanjit keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Sanjit saga began at 2023 South CRC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sanjit plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Sanjit has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sanjit has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sanjit showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 South OLC in BS U11, which means Sanjit traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Sanjit keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sanjit has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sanjit, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

sankeerthi_marasani.name: Sankeerthi Marasani
sankeerthi_marasani.summary:
A Georgia competitor, Sankeerthi Marasani broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 46 entries across 16 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Sankeerthi is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Sankeerthi competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Sankeerthi adapts to anyone:
  • Meghna Yeluguri (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (8 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Yashas Cheerlavancha (current primary partner) — 10 events, 1 title together in XD, spanning 3 seasons

Sankeerthi has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Sankeerthi isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Sankeerthi has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
sankeerthi_marasani.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Sankeerthi Marasani, from Georgia, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sankeerthi has 46 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sankeerthi saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2024 South CRC (TBTT) in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sankeerthi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 17 entries — a stretch where Sankeerthi actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sankeerthi is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Sankeerthi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Sankeerthi has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sankeerthi does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Sankeerthi has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Sankeerthi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sankeerthi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sankeerthi is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sankeerthi and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sankeerthi needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Sankeerthi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sankeerthi's texts. The go-to partner is Yashas Cheerlavancha (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Sriyansh Chigullapally has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sankeerthi's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Sankeerthi has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Sankeerthi has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Sankeerthi has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sankeerthi has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sankeerthi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Sankeerthi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Sankeerthi entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Sankeerthi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sankeerthi's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 45 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sankeerthi is a player who chokes in finals like it is a paid hobby. Will Sankeerthi figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sankeerthi's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sanskruti_kulkarni.roast:
Step right up and meet Sanskruti Kulkarni, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 19 career entries in 8 tournaments, Sanskruti has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Sanskruti drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 19 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sanskruti saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 1 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sanskruti plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. A round-of-17 exit at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U19 — we hope Viswanath Jay Nair has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sanskruti entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sanskruti's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sanskruti and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sanskruti is not just losing locally — Sanskruti is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kulkarni family travels cross-country so Sanskruti can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sanskruti has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sanskruti showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U19, which means Sanskruti traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NW ORC in GS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sanskruti keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sanskruti has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sanskruti knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

Sarah is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Sarah wants to be. sarah_xie.name: Sarah Xie
sarah_xie.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Sarah Xie has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U13 level, capturing 2 titles across 7 tournaments and 15 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Sarah keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Sarah has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Sarah especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Sarah has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Sahasra Nali (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Peter Lei (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Sarah is still ascending.

Sarah has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Sarah's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
sarah_xie.roast:
Allow us to introduce Sarah Xie, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 wins across 15 entries in 7 tournaments. That is a 13% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Sarah shows up to a tournament, Sarah pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Sarah saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sarah's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 8 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sarah plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sarah can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Sarah has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Sahasra Nali (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Pradhyumn Surendran has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sarah's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Sarah has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Sarah has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sarah has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sarah showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11, which means Sarah traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Sarah entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Sarah paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Sarah story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 2 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. But every season Sarah comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Sarah, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

sarvesh_hariharasudhan.roast:

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sarvesh saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sarvesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Sarvesh has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Sarvesh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sarvesh has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sarvesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Sarvesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Sarvesh entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Sarvesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sarvesh keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sarvesh has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sarvesh knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

sasha_suman.name: Sasha Suman
sasha_suman.summary:
Out of Virginia, Sasha Suman is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (GD U17) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 8 career titles across 35 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Notably, Sasha has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Sasha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, 7 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (GD U17, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 GD) • Junior Nationals champion (XD U17, GD U17) • 4 ORC titles.

Sasha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 19 different players across the career:
  • Yalan Zhu (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 5 titles, building serious momentum (13 total events in GD/U17, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Vivek Rai (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Adhyaan Balaji — a trusted XD partner (8 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)

Having moved up to U19, Sasha is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Sasha brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Sasha has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Sasha has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 13 seeded entries across the career, Sasha consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 6 seasons and 8 titles, Sasha has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
sasha_suman.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Sasha Suman, from Virginia, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Sasha is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (GD U17, Women's Singles), which means Sasha has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Sasha's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 89 career entries to discuss and a 9% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Sasha saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sasha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 6 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Sasha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sasha is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Here is a fun detail: in GS U17, Sasha used to be seeded #1 (at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC)). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19)). Tournament committees are literally telling Sasha 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

If Sasha's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Sasha has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Sasha does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Sasha has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 8 wins means Sasha is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sasha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sasha is 0-for-28. Zero titles in 28 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sasha and suddenly the wins start appearing — 8 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sasha needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Sasha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sasha's texts. The most successful partnership is with Yalan Zhu — 13 events, 5 titles — and honestly, Yalan Zhu deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Yalan Zhu a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Adhyaan Balaji has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sasha entered 12 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sasha's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sasha and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Sasha has been seeded in 13 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Sasha takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in GS U17, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Sasha. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Sasha has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sasha is not just losing locally — Sasha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Suman family travels cross-country so Sasha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sasha has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sasha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17, which means Sasha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), where Sasha entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Sasha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 8 titles, 89 entries, and 81 that ended without a trophy. A player who has a finals record that should come with a trigger warning. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the moments of brilliance — Sasha looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Sasha. We will be watching.

sashanth_senthil.roast:
Allow us to introduce Sashanth Senthil, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 56 career entries in 23 tournaments, Sashanth has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 55 entries where Sashanth drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 56 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sashanth saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

In Boys' Doubles, Sashanth has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sashanth chokes in Boys' Doubles finals specifically.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Sashanth experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Sashanth has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC, 2024 DFW South OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Sashanth has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sashanth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 56 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Sashanth has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sashanth's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Niranjan Manjith: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Niranjan Manjith has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Charvi Murali has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sashanth entered 4 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sashanth's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sashanth and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Sashanth has shown up 5 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sashanth is not just losing locally — Sashanth is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Senthil family travels cross-country so Sashanth can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sashanth has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sashanth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Sashanth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Sashanth entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Sashanth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

5 seasons. 56 entries. Zero titles. If this were a stock, the SEC would have delisted it by now. If this were a restaurant, the health inspector would have questions. If this were a dating profile, the app would gently suggest 'trying a different approach.' But this is junior badminton, where hope springs eternal and so does Sashanth's entry form. We salute the commitment. We question the strategy. We will absolutely be back next season to see if anything changes. (It will not.)

sasikavi_muthu.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Sasikavi Muthu, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sasikavi has 17 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sasikavi saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

If Sasikavi's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Sasikavi has reached the finals 2 times — at 2025 Egret Midwest OLC, 2026 Play N Thrive MW CRC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Sasikavi has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sasikavi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Sasikavi has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Lucas Lu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Lucas Lu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Sasikavi has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Sasikavi has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Sasikavi has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sasikavi has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sasikavi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Sasikavi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Sasikavi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 49th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Sasikavi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Sasikavi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sasikavi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sasikavi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

Step right up and meet Saujas Patil, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 27 career entries in 11 tournaments, Saujas has 3 titles — a 11% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 8 entries where Saujas drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 27 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Saujas saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC, where Saujas burst onto the scene with a title in BD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Saujas has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Saujas promised and what Saujas has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 11 entries — a stretch where Saujas actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Saujas is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Saujas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Saujas can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Saujas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Saujas's texts. The go-to partner is Aaron Brijesh (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U17 — we hope Stella Pan has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Saujas's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Saujas entered 2 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Saujas's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Saujas and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Saujas has shown up 5 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Saujas has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Saujas is not just losing locally — Saujas is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Patil family travels cross-country so Saujas can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Saujas has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Saujas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U17, which means Saujas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 South Frisco ORC, where Saujas entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Saujas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Saujas's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 24 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Saujas is a player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. Will Saujas figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Saujas's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

savannah_h_kam.name: Savannah H Kam
savannah_h_kam.summary:
