Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Savannah's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Savannah especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Savannah adapts to anyone:

  • Riya Suresh (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons

The 2023-2024 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Savannah is still ascending.

Savannah has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Savannah has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Savannah consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 3 titles, Savannah has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.

The Selection committee looked at Savannah's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Savannah and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Savannah has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Savannah is not just losing locally — Savannah is losing NATIONALLY. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. scott_lin.name: Scott Lin
scott_lin.summary:
Scott Lin is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (BD U11, XD U13, BS U11, BS U13, XD U11) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 4 career titles across 2 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

A true triple-threat, Scott competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (BD U11, XD U13, BS U11, BS U13, XD U11, 2017, 2019).

Scott has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Vera Lin (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #3)
  • Linden Wang — 2 events in BD, producing 2 titles together
  • Jasmine Yeung — 2 events in XD, producing 2 titles together

Having moved up to U13, Scott is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Scott brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Scott has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
scott_lin.roast:
Allow us to introduce Scott Lin, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U13 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Scott is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist (BD U11, XD U13, BS U11, BS U13, XD U11), which means Scott has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Scott's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. A Pan Am gold medal. International champion. The real deal. We would love to end the story right here, but unfortunately there are 8 career entries to discuss and a 50% overall win rate that suggests the Pan Am gold was the peak of a very pointy mountain with a LOT of valley on either side. Let's dig in.

The Scott saga began at 2017 Pan Am Junior, where Scott burst onto the scene with a title in BD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Scott has delivered on some of those promises: 4 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Scott promised and what Scott has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2016-2017 season — 4 titles in 5 entries — a stretch where Scott actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Scott is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Scott plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Scott is 0-for-2. Zero titles in 2 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Scott and suddenly the wins start appearing — 4 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Scott needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

The Scott story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 4 titles across 2 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Scott comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Scott, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

scott_wang_lin.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Scott Wang Lin file just landed on our desk. Scott hails from California, currently competing at the U17 level. An Open Regional Championship titlist with 1 ORC title, which sounds very official — and it is, ORCs are the real deal. But let's zoom out: that's 1 wins out of 13 ORC entries, and an overall career of 2-for-16 (12%). Regional royalty, national afterthought. A big deal on the local circuit, a footnote everywhere else. Let's dig in.

The Scott saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC, where Scott burst onto the scene with a title in BD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Scott has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Scott promised and what Scott has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Scott actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Scott is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Scott would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Scott has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Scott has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Scott's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Scott plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Scott is 0-for-6. Zero titles in 6 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Scott and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Scott needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Scott has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Scott is not just losing locally — Scott is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lin family travels cross-country so Scott can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Scott has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Scott showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17, which means Scott traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Scott entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Scott paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Scott's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 14 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Scott is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Scott figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Scott's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sean_choudhury.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Sean Choudhury file just landed on our desk. Sean hails from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Sean's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Sean keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Sean saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Sean has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Sean traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Sean entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Sean paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sean keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sean has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sean knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

sean_li.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Sean Li, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 37 career entries in 15 tournaments, Sean has 1 title — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 36 entries where Sean drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 37 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sean saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sean's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 22 entries — a stretch where Sean actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sean is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sean plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sean is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sean and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sean needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Sean has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sean's texts. The go-to partner is Tamara Valueva (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Yichen Li has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sean's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sean entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sean's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sean and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Sean has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sean is not just losing locally — Sean is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Sean can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sean has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sean showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Sean traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Sean entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Sean paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title, 37 entries, and 36 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Sean looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Sean. We will be watching.

seanna_yang.name: Seanna Yang
seanna_yang.summary:
Hailing from Washington, Seanna Yang is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Seanna has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Seanna competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 1 ORC title.

Seanna has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Derek Huang (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (15 total events in XD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Grace Eunhye Park (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (11 total events in GD, spanning 4 seasons)

Seanna has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Seanna has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 4 titles, Seanna has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
seanna_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Seanna Yang, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Seanna has 42 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 10% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Seanna saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Seanna's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 16 entries — a stretch where Seanna actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Can we talk about Seanna and Girls' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 1 losses. Seanna can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Seanna still cannot reach it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Seanna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Seanna can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. The go-to partner is Derek Huang (15 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U13 — we hope Derek Huang has emotionally recovered.

Of Seanna's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Seanna feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals, Seanna has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Seanna has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Seanna is not just losing locally — Seanna is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Seanna can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Seanna has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Seanna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11, which means Seanna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Seanna entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Seanna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Seanna's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 4 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 38 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Seanna is a player who has turned Girls' Singles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. Will Seanna figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Seanna's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

  • Terence Cai (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD)
  • Katie Wong (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Selena is still ascending.

Selena has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Selena has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Selena consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Selena is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
selena_wei.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Selena Wei, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 84 career entries in 30 tournaments, Selena has 6 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 13 entries where Selena drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 84 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Selena saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Selena burst onto the scene with a title in XD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Selena has delivered on some of those promises: 6 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Selena promised and what Selena has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 23 entries — a stretch where Selena actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 14 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

In XD U15, Selena was once THE player to beat — 2 titles including at 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC. These days, Selena is finishing 2nd, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date.

Girls' Singles finals and Selena — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Selena has turned 2 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Selena's trophies to save time.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Selena experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Selena has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Selena does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Selena has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 6 wins means Selena is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Selena plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Selena is 0-for-25. Zero titles in 25 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Selena and suddenly the wins start appearing — 6 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Selena needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Selena has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Selena's texts. The go-to partner is Jacob Zhou (11 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Cayden Wang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Selena entered 9 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Selena's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Selena and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Selena has competed 9 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

One thing nobody can accuse Selena of is playing it safe. Selena has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Selena has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Selena has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Selena is not just losing locally — Selena is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wei family travels cross-country so Selena can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Selena has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Selena showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U13, which means Selena traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Selena entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Selena paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Selena's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 6 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 78 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Selena is a player who former champion currently in witness protection from their own legacy. Will Selena figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Selena's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

Selina has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Selina has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Selina is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
selina_lei.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Selina Lei, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 win across 42 entries in 15 tournaments. That is a 2% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Selina shows up to a tournament, Selina pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Selina saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 MassBad NE OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Selina's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 19 entries — a stretch where Selina actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Selina is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Selina plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Selina is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Selina and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Selina needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 22 of them, to be precise. Selina has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Selina's texts. The go-to partner is Benson Liang (3 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Devin Shao has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Selina's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Selina has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Selina is not just losing locally — Selina is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lei family travels cross-country so Selina can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Selina has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Selina showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Selina traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Selina entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Selina paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Selina story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Selina comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Selina, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

selina_xu.name: Selina Xu
selina_xu.summary:
Out of California, Selina Xu stands as an ORC champion, capturing an impressive 3 ORC titles across 3 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Selina as one of the premier players on the circuit.

Notably, Selina has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Selina is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 3 ORC titles.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Selina boasts 29 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a positive +223 point differential across the career, a solid 62% win rate over 66 matches.

Selina has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Angela Wang (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (10 total events in GD/U17, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Hanyi Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ethan Wu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Selina is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Selina has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Selina has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Selina consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 4 titles, Selina has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
selina_xu.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Selina Xu, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 70 career entries in 25 tournaments, Selina has 4 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 16 entries where Selina drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 70 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Selina saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Selina's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Selina actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 13 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Girls' Doubles finals and Selina — name a more iconic tragedy. 6 trips to the championship match, and Selina has turned 4 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Selina's trophies to save time. In Mixed Doubles, Selina has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Selina chokes in Mixed Doubles finals specifically.

If Selina's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Selina has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Selina entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Selina does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Selina has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 4 wins means Selina is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Selina plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Selina can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Selina has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Selina's texts. The go-to partner is Angela Wang (10 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-97 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Brendan Chen has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Selina entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Selina's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Selina and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Selina has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Then there is the seeding situation. Selina has been seeded in 8 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Selina takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, and finishing 97th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Selina. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Selina has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Selina is not just losing locally — Selina is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xu family travels cross-country so Selina can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Selina has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Selina showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, which means Selina traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-97 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Selina entered 3 events with results ranging from 49th to 97th. That is an average placement of 81st, which is a polite way of saying Selina paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles, 70 entries, and 66 that ended without a trophy. A player who has turned Girls' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Selina looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Selina. We will be watching.

seojin_lee.name: Seojin Lee
seojin_lee.summary:
Hailing from California, Seojin Lee is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U17 level, with 3 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Seojin competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
seojin_lee.roast:
Allow us to introduce Seojin Lee, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Seojin is right on schedule. The Seojin saga began at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Seojin has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Seojin has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Seojin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17, which means Seojin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Seojin entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. seoyoung_lee.name: Seoyoung Lee
seoyoung_lee.summary:
Out of Illinois, Seoyoung Lee has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 39 entries across 14 tournaments, Seoyoung is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Seoyoung is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Seoyoung is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Seoyoung adapts to anyone:
  • Anjika Pattojoshi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (7 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Shaan V Singh (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Aditya Prasad Iyer (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #3)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 3 tournaments. Seoyoung is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Seoyoung has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Seoyoung brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
seoyoung_lee.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Seoyoung Lee, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 39 events across 14 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 39-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Seoyoung's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Seoyoung keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Seoyoung saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Seoyoung would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 7 times Seoyoung has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 7 times Seoyoung has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Seoyoung's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Seoyoung plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 39 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Seoyoung has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Seoyoung's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Anjika Pattojoshi: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Anjika Pattojoshi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Shanmukh Ayyalasomayajula has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Seoyoung of is playing it safe. Seoyoung has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Seoyoung has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Seoyoung has built a permanent residence on that line. Seoyoung has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Seoyoung has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Seoyoung showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Seoyoung traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Seoyoung entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Seoyoung paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 5 seasons and 39 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Seoyoung is not most humans. Seoyoung is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Seoyoung the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Seoyoung. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Serena has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Serena is not just losing locally — Serena is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Serena can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Serena has 31 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Serena showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Serena traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Serena entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Serena paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles, 45 entries, and 43 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Serena looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Serena. We will be watching.
