serena_yang.name: Serena Yang
serena_yang.summary:
Out of Texas, Serena Yang is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U19 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Notably, Serena has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Serena is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries).

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Serena adapts to anyone:
  • Srikrishna Sriganesh — a trusted XD partner (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Geoffray Lee — a trusted U17/XD partner (5 events, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Serena has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Serena has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Serena brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
serena_yang.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Serena Yang, from Texas, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 49 career entries in 19 tournaments, Serena has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 48 entries where Serena drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 49 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Serena saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Serena plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 49 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Serena has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Serena's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Geoffray Lee: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Geoffray Lee has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U15 — we hope Darren Teu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Serena entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Serena's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Serena and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Serena has shown up 8 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Serena has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Serena is not just losing locally — Serena is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Serena can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Serena has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Serena showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Serena traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Serena entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Serena paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 5 seasons and 49 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Serena is not most humans. Serena is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Serena the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Serena. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

serenity_anne_foley.name: Serenity Anne Foley
serenity_anne_foley.summary: Representing North Carolina, Serenity Anne Foley has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Serenity's.
serenity_anne_foley.roast:
Step right up and meet Serenity Anne Foley, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Serenity is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 South OLC (TBTT), and so far, the only thing Serenity has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Serenity saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Serenity is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Serenity's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Serenity is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

With only 1 entries on the books, Serenity is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Serenity. We will have more material. We always do.

seth_li.name: Seth Li
seth_li.summary:
Hailing from New Jersey, Seth Li is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (BD U15). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Seth in rarified international company.

Seth is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Seth is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 9 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (BD U15, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 BS, U13 BD, U13 XD, U15 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BD U13) • 5 ORC titles.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Seth boasts a clutch 55% win rate in three-game matches (11/20), a career +728 point differential, a biggest win margin of 34 points.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Seth adapts to anyone:
  • Terence Cai (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (11 total events in BD/U15, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Emily Min Tang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U15/XD (8 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Alexander Gu (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Seth has competed across U11, U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Seth has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Seth has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 15 titles, Seth is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
seth_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Seth Li, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Seth is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (BD U15), which means Seth has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Seth's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Seth went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Seth represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 98 career entries and a 15% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 98 entries worth of material.

The Seth saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 NE CRC in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Seth's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 5 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Seth actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 11 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

In BD U15, Seth was once THE player to beat — 1 title including at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. These days, Seth is finishing 2nd, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date. Remember when Seth used to win BD U17? Because Seth sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Seth took gold at 2025 NE MassBad OLC, and since then? Best finish: 3rd. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left.

In Mixed Doubles, Seth has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 4 championship appearances, 3 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Seth chokes in Mixed Doubles finals specifically. In Boys' Singles, Seth has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Seth chokes in Boys' Singles finals specifically.

The seeding trajectory in BS U17 tells a story: #1 at 2023 NE OLC (NVBC), down to #2 at 2025 NE MassBad OLC. When the people whose job is to rank you start ranking you lower, that is not a slump — that is a trend line with a very clear direction, and it is not pointing up.

If Seth's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Seth has reached 11 championship matches across this career. That is 11 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 11 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (8 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Seth does own 15 actual titles, so it is not like Seth has never won anything. But 11 finals losses versus 15 wins means Seth is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Seth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 9 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Seth can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Seth has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Seth's texts. The go-to partner is Kimaya Krishna (18 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Terence Cai has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Seth has been seeded in 27 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Seth takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 NE OLC (NVBC) in BS U17, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Seth. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Seth of is playing it safe. Seth has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Seth has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 15 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Seth has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Seth is not just losing locally — Seth is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Seth can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Seth has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Seth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Seth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Seth entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 34th, which is a polite way of saying Seth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 15 titles, 98 entries, and 83 that ended without a trophy. A player who proof that glory has an expiration date and Seth's is past due. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the moments of brilliance — Seth looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Seth. We will be watching.

Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Seungwoo, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

  • Pratham Bansal — a trusted BD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

Shaan has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Shaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Shaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The sport needs people like Shaan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Shaan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shaan has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shaan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U17, which means Shaan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Shaan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Shaan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 17 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Shaan is not most humans. Shaan is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Shaan the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Shaan. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

What makes Shagun especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Someone get the popcorn, because the Shagun preet Kaur file just landed on our desk. Shagun hails from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Shagun is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19), and so far, the only thing Shagun has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Shagun saga began at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shagun plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

With only 3 entries on the books, Shagun is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Shagun. We will have more material. We always do.

shagunpreet_kaur.name: Shagunpreet Kaur
shagunpreet_kaur.summary:
Representing North Carolina, Shagunpreet Kaur is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (GS U19) puts Shagunpreet in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Shagunpreet is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, a doubles best of #3, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (GS U19) • 1 ORC title.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Shagunpreet adapts to anyone:
  • Alan Premkumar (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Rishita Tubati (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Kavya Krishna (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #7)
  • Surya Ilamurugu (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

Shagunpreet is peaking at the right time in 2023-2024, with 3 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Shagunpreet is still ascending.

Shagunpreet has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Shagunpreet's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
shagunpreet_kaur.roast:
Step right up and meet Shagunpreet Kaur, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Shagunpreet has 23 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 13% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shagunpreet saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in GS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shagunpreet's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2023-2024 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 14 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shagunpreet plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Shagunpreet can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Shagunpreet has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shagunpreet's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alan Premkumar: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alan Premkumar has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Alan Premkumar has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Shagunpreet has competed 6 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shagunpreet is not just losing locally — Shagunpreet is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kaur family travels cross-country so Shagunpreet can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shagunpreet has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shagunpreet showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19, which means Shagunpreet traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Shagunpreet entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Shagunpreet paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Shagunpreet's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 20 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Shagunpreet is a player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. Will Shagunpreet figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Shagunpreet's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

Hailing from Illinois, Shanmukh Ayyalasomayajula broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 39 entries across 16 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Shanmukh is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Shanmukh is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Mohammad Tahmid Hossain (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Kaitlyn Kim (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Shanmukh is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Shanmukh has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Shanmukh isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Shanmukh is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
shanmukh_ayyalasomayajula.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Shanmukh Ayyalasomayajula, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Shanmukh has 39 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shanmukh saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shanmukh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Shanmukh would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Shanmukh has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Shanmukh has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Shanmukh's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shanmukh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Shanmukh is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Shanmukh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Shanmukh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Shanmukh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shanmukh's texts. The go-to partner is Mohammad Tahmid Hossain (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17 — we hope Aryan Dayaram has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Shanmukh's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Shanmukh has shown up 4 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Shanmukh of is playing it safe. Shanmukh has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Shanmukh has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Shanmukh has built a permanent residence on that line. Shanmukh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shanmukh has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shanmukh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Shanmukh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shanmukh entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 45th, which is a polite way of saying Shanmukh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title, 39 entries, and 38 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Shanmukh looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Shanmukh. We will be watching.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shanyu plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 33 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Shanyu has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shanyu's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Siya Chavarkar has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Shanyu has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Shanyu of is playing it safe. Shanyu has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17 — which means Shanyu has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Shanyu has built a permanent residence on that line. Shanyu has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shanyu has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shanyu showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Shanyu traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shanyu entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 97th. That is an average placement of 65th, which is a polite way of saying Shanyu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 33 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Shanyu. Shanyu just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Shanyu has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Shanyu. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Shanyu: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

shao_lang_qin.roast:

Definitely.

shaoxuan_gong.name: Shaoxuan Gong
shaoxuan_gong.summary:
Hailing from California, Shaoxuan Gong has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Shaoxuan's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Shaoxuan has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Shaoxuan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Shaoxuan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 21 different players across the career:
  • Mokshagna Surpur (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Daiwik Reddy (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Shaoxuan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Shaoxuan has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Shaoxuan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
shaoxuan_gong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Shaoxuan Gong, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Shaoxuan has 41 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shaoxuan saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 OBA NW OLC in GS U11 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shaoxuan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 8 entries — a stretch where Shaoxuan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Shaoxuan is 0-for-13. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Can we talk about Shaoxuan and Mixed Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Shaoxuan can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Mixed Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Shaoxuan still cannot reach it.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Shaoxuan's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Shaoxuan has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 OBA NW OLC, where Shaoxuan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Shaoxuan does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Shaoxuan has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Shaoxuan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shaoxuan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Shaoxuan is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Shaoxuan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shaoxuan's texts. The go-to partner is Satvikram Parvathaneni (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11 — we hope Satvikram Parvathaneni has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Shaoxuan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Shaoxuan has shown up 5 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Shaoxuan has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shaoxuan is not just losing locally — Shaoxuan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Gong family travels cross-country so Shaoxuan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shaoxuan has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shaoxuan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, which means Shaoxuan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shaoxuan entered 2 events with results ranging from 49th to 49th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Shaoxuan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Shaoxuan story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has turned Mixed Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. But every season Shaoxuan comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Shaoxuan, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Shashank Vandavasi, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 19 events across 10 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 19-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Shashank's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Shashank keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Shashank saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shashank plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On the doubles front, Shashank has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U15 — we hope Alex Jiang has emotionally recovered.

Shashank has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shashank has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shashank showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Shashank traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Shashank paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

4 seasons. 19 entries. Zero titles. If this were a stock, the SEC would have delisted it by now. If this were a restaurant, the health inspector would have questions. If this were a dating profile, the app would gently suggest 'trying a different approach.' But this is junior badminton, where hope springs eternal and so does Shashank's entry form. We salute the commitment. We question the strategy. We will absolutely be back next season to see if anything changes. (It will not.)

Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 20 career entries in 10 tournaments, Shaunak has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 19 entries where Shaunak drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 20 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Shaunak saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shaunak plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Shaunak has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shaunak's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U17 — we hope Veda Gayatri Mullangi has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Shaunak entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Shaunak's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Shaunak and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Shaunak has shown up 4 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Shaunak has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shaunak has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shaunak showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U17, which means Shaunak traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U17 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 U.S. Selection Event, where Shaunak entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Shaunak paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 20 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Shaunak. Shaunak just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Shaunak has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Shaunak. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Shaunak: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

shaurya_agrawal.roast:
Step right up and meet Shaurya Agrawal, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Shaurya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Shaurya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Shaurya saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shaurya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Shaurya has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Rasika Gokhale (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Nethmi Hewawitharana has emotionally recovered.

Shaurya has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shaurya has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shaurya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Shaurya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Shaurya entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Shaurya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Shaurya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Shaurya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Shaurya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

shaurya_maddela.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Shaurya Maddela file just landed on our desk. Shaurya hails from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 68 career entries in 27 tournaments, Shaurya has 2 titles — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 33 entries where Shaurya drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 68 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Shaurya saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in BS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shaurya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Shaurya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Shaurya is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Shaurya would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Shaurya has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Shaurya has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Shaurya's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shaurya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Shaurya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 26 of them, to be precise. Shaurya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shaurya's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Yeewei Yap: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Yeewei Yap has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Wayne Chien has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Shaurya's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Shaurya entered 4 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Shaurya's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Shaurya and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Shaurya has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shaurya is not just losing locally — Shaurya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Maddela family travels cross-country so Shaurya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shaurya has 29 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shaurya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Shaurya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shaurya entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Shaurya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Shaurya's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 66 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Shaurya is a player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. Will Shaurya figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Shaurya's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

shawn_xia.summary:
Out of New Jersey, Shawn Xia has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in BD U19. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Shawn belongs with the best in the country.

A true triple-threat, Shawn competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (8 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (BD U19) • 1 ORC title.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Shawn adapts to anyone:
  • Chris Wu (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (17 total events in BD/U17/U19, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Kavya Krishna (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Mikayla Cruz — 2 events, 1 title together in XD

Shawn has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Shawn has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 3 titles, Shawn has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
shawn_xia.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Shawn Xia, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 47 career entries in 18 tournaments, Shawn has 3 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 14 entries where Shawn drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 47 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Shawn saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2023 NE OLC (NVBC) in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shawn's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Shawn actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 3 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Shawn's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Shawn has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Shawn entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U19, BD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Shawn does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Shawn has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 3 wins means Shawn is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shawn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Shawn is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Shawn and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Shawn needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Shawn has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shawn's texts. The go-to partner is Chris Wu (17 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BD U17 — we hope Chris Wu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Shawn entered 8 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Shawn's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Shawn and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Shawn 5 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shawn is not just losing locally — Shawn is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xia family travels cross-country so Shawn can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shawn has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shawn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Shawn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), where Shawn entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Shawn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Shawn's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 44 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Shawn is a player who has a finals record that should come with a trigger warning. Will Shawn figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Shawn's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

shawn_ye.summary:

shawn_ye.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Shawn Ye, from parts unknown, continues to exist, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Shawn is right on schedule. It all started at 2026 CBA Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Shawn has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Shawn saga began at 2026 CBA Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

With only 3 entries on the books, Shawn is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Shawn. We will have more material. We always do.

Sheehan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Sheehan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Sheehan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.
