sherlyn_mei.name: Sherlyn Mei
sherlyn_mei.summary:
A California competitor, Sherlyn Mei has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Sherlyn's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Sherlyn has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Sherlyn especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-4 finisher.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Sherlyn adapts to anyone:
  • Jonathan Loh (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Irene Xu (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)

Sherlyn has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Sherlyn has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Sherlyn brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sherlyn_mei.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Sherlyn Mei, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 wins across 21 entries in 7 tournaments. That is a 10% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Sherlyn shows up to a tournament, Sherlyn pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Sherlyn saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sherlyn's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sherlyn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sherlyn can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Sherlyn has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Jonathan Loh: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jonathan Loh has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Ethan Xiao-ze Yang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sherlyn's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Sherlyn has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sherlyn is not just losing locally — Sherlyn is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mei family travels cross-country so Sherlyn can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sherlyn has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sherlyn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U13, which means Sherlyn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Sherlyn entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Sherlyn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Sherlyn story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. But every season Sherlyn comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Sherlyn, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

shinher_jiang.roast:

This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 19-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Shinher's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Shinher keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Shinher saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

The go-to partner is Emily Ou (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13 — we hope Morgan Li has emotionally recovered.

That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Shinher paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Shinher keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Shinher has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Shinher, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

shirin_shaik.name: Shirin Shaik
shirin_shaik.summary:
Representing California, Shirin Shaik is relentlessly knocking on the door, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching 3 finals across 49 entries. A player who reaches that many championship matches has all the tools — the breakthrough title is coming.

What makes Shirin especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Shirin has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Daiwik Reddy (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (10 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Arshiya Raheja (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Shirin has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Shirin has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Shirin brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
shirin_shaik.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Shirin Shaik, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Shirin has 49 career entries across 18 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shirin saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Girls' Doubles finals and Shirin — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Shirin has turned 2 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Shirin's trophies to save time.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Shirin's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Shirin has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships, where Shirin entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U15, GD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. And remember: Shirin has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shirin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 49 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Shirin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shirin's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Daiwik Reddy: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Daiwik Reddy has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Jason Yiheng Luo has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Shirin has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shirin is not just losing locally — Shirin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shaik family travels cross-country so Shirin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shirin has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shirin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15, which means Shirin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Shirin entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Shirin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 49 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Shirin. Shirin just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Shirin has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Shirin. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Shirin: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shivali saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shivali plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 11 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Shivali has shown up 4 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Shivali has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shivali has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shivali showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17, which means Shivali traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shivali entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Shivali paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 11 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Shivali. Shivali just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Shivali has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Shivali. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Shivali: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

shivani_ramakrishnan.name: Shivani Ramakrishnan
shivani_ramakrishnan.summary:
Representing California, Shivani Ramakrishnan has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U17 level, capturing 2 titles across 22 tournaments and 56 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Shivani keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Notably, Shivani has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Shivani especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 21 different doubles partners over the years, Shivani adapts to anyone:
  • Felicia Ho (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Zachary Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Aaron Lin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #4)

Shivani has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Shivani has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 2 titles, Shivani is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
shivani_ramakrishnan.roast:
Step right up and meet Shivani Ramakrishnan, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Shivani has 56 career entries across 22 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shivani saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shivani's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Shivani would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Shivani has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Shivani has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Shivani's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shivani plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Shivani can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Shivani has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shivani's texts. The go-to partner is Felicia Ho (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Yooseung Jung has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Shivani's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Shivani has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Shivani has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shivani is not just losing locally — Shivani is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ramakrishnan family travels cross-country so Shivani can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shivani has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shivani showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13, which means Shivani traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Shivani entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Shivani paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Shivani story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. But every season Shivani comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Shivani, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Krishna Kuchi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U15 — we hope Reyansh Sehgal has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Shlok has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Shlok has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shlok has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shlok showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Shlok traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Shlok entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Shlok paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Shlok keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Shlok has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Shlok knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

shloka_edunuri.name: Shloka Edunuri
shloka_edunuri.summary:
Representing Virginia, Shloka Edunuri knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 13 entries across 5 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Shloka has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Shloka especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Shloka has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Akshith P Arava (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Ruohan Joy Du (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)

The 2023-2024 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Shloka is still ascending.

Shloka has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Shloka has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Shloka's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
shloka_edunuri.roast:
Step right up and meet Shloka Edunuri, from Virginia, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 win across 13 entries in 5 tournaments. That is a 8% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Shloka shows up to a tournament, Shloka pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Shloka saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shloka's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shloka plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Shloka is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. On the doubles front, Shloka has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Akshith P Arava has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Shloka's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Shloka has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shloka is not just losing locally — Shloka is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Edunuri family travels cross-country so Shloka can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shloka has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shloka showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Shloka traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Shloka entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Shloka paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Shloka's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 12 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. Will Shloka figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Shloka's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

At Junior Nationals, Shloka has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Shloka paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Not a trophy, obviously — Shloka knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Shloka changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

shraddha_prakash.name: Shraddha Prakash
shraddha_prakash.summary:
Out of Texas, Shraddha Prakash wears the gold from Junior Nationals — the single most important tournament for any American junior player in GD U15, GS U15. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 21 career victories across 26 tournaments, Shraddha has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Shraddha is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Shraddha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 9 singles titles, 7 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (GD U15, GS U15) • 8 ORC titles.

Diving into the match data reveals: Shraddha boasts a clutch 62% win rate in three-game matches (13/21), an extraordinary 87% match win rate across 94 matches, a biggest win margin of 41 points.

Shraddha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 21 different players across the career:
  • Lexuan Wen (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (6 total events in GD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Srinivas Raghav Subash (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Adhyaan Balaji (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Radhika Birur (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 10 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Shraddha is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Shraddha has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Shraddha has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Shraddha has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 21 titles, Shraddha is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
shraddha_prakash.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Shraddha Prakash, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won GD U15, GS U15. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 66 career entries across 26 tournaments, 21 total wins, and 45 losses. A 32% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Shraddha has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Shraddha saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shraddha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 10 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Shraddha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 6 wins in 11 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

XD U15 used to be Shraddha's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai era. Now Shraddha is grinding for a top-2 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Shraddha reaches a final. Shraddha has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Shraddha does own 21 actual titles, so it is not like Shraddha has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 21 wins means Shraddha is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shraddha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 9 singles, 7 doubles, 5 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Shraddha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 21 of them, to be precise. Shraddha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shraddha's texts. The most successful partnership is with Lexuan Wen — 6 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Lexuan Wen deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Lexuan Wen a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Mikael Chang has emotionally recovered.

Of Shraddha's 21 career titles, 11 came at local events versus 10 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Shraddha feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room.

One thing nobody can accuse Shraddha of is playing it safe. Shraddha has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Shraddha has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 19 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Shraddha has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shraddha is not just losing locally — Shraddha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 32% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Prakash family travels cross-country so Shraddha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shraddha has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shraddha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 South ORC in XD U13, which means Shraddha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-25 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Shraddha entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Shraddha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 21 titles, 66 entries, and 45 that ended without a trophy. A player who former champion currently in witness protection from their own legacy. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the moments of brilliance — Shraddha looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Shraddha. We will be watching.

shradha_koppisetti.name: Shradha Koppisetti
shradha_koppisetti.summary:
Out of Illinois, Shradha Koppisetti has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 16 entries in 6 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

What makes Shradha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Shradha has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Shradha isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Shradha's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
shradha_koppisetti.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Shradha Koppisetti, from Illinois, continues to exist, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Shradha has 16 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shradha saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shradha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 16 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Shradha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shradha's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U17 — we hope Arshad Ali Shaik has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Shradha has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Shradha has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shradha has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shradha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in GS U15, which means Shradha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shradha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Shradha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Shradha keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Shradha has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Shradha, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

A California competitor, Shravan Chockalingam is a multi-time champion on the junior circuit, currently competing at the U13 level, capturing 2 titles across 15 tournaments and 40 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Shravan keeps finding ways to come out on top.

A true triple-threat, Shravan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

Shravan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Arundhathi Padma Mohan (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (14 total events in U13/XD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Pranav Vijay (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U13 (5 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Rocky Khuu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

Shravan is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 2 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Shravan is still ascending.

Shravan has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Shravan has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 2 titles, Shravan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
shravan_chockalingam.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Shravan Chockalingam file just landed on our desk. Shravan hails from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 40 career entries in 15 tournaments, Shravan has 2 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 19 entries where Shravan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 40 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Shravan saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shravan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 13 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shravan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Shravan is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Shravan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Shravan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Shravan has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Arundhathi Padma Mohan (14 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Pranav Vijay has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Shravan's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Shravan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Shravan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Shravan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Shravan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shravan is not just losing locally — Shravan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chockalingam family travels cross-country so Shravan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shravan has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shravan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Shravan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Shravan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Shravan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Shravan's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 38 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Shravan is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Shravan figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Shravan's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

shreya_rajesh_kumar.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Shreya Rajesh Kumar, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 39 entries in 13 tournaments. That is a 3% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Shreya shows up to a tournament, Shreya pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Shreya saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in GD U11 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shreya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 15 entries — a stretch where Shreya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Shreya is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In Girls' Doubles, Shreya has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Shreya chokes in Girls' Doubles finals specifically.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Shreya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Shreya has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Shreya does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Shreya has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Shreya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shreya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Shreya is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Shreya and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Shreya needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Shreya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shreya's texts. The go-to partner is Venba Senkadir (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Ritvik Singh has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Shreya's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shreya is not just losing locally — Shreya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kumar family travels cross-country so Shreya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shreya has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shreya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11, which means Shreya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Shreya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Shreya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title, 39 entries, and 38 that ended without a trophy. A player who the undisputed champion of second place in Girls' Doubles. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Shreya looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Shreya. We will be watching.

shreya_ranawat.name: Shreya Ranawat
shreya_ranawat.summary: Shreya Ranawat is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Shreya's.
shreya_ranawat.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Shreya Ranawat, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Shreya is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 NW OLC, and so far, the only thing Shreya has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Shreya saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Shreya is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Shreya's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Shreya is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Shreya is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Shreya wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

shreya_s_kirikera.name: Shreya S Kirikera
shreya_s_kirikera.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Shreya S Kirikera has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U15 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 38 entries in 14 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Shreya is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Shreya adapts to anyone:
  • Lucas Liu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Tanishka Hande (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Aarush Mali — a trusted XD partner (8 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Shreya has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Shreya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
shreya_s_kirikera.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Shreya S Kirikera, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Shreya has 38 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shreya saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shreya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 38 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Shreya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shreya's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Aarush Mali: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aarush Mali has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Gagan Polineni has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Shreya has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shreya has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shreya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Shreya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shreya entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 49th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Shreya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 38 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Shreya is not most humans. Shreya is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Shreya the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Shreya. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Shriariman has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shriariman's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Nidhi Sujin: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Nidhi Sujin has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Varshini Subramanian has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Shriariman has shown up 9 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shriariman is not just losing locally — Shriariman is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Karthigayan family travels cross-country so Shriariman can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shriariman has 38 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shriariman showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Shriariman traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shriariman entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 49th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Shriariman paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 54 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Shriariman. Shriariman just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Shriariman has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Shriariman. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Shriariman: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

shrihan_reddy_pingili.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Shrihan Reddy Pingili file just landed on our desk. Shrihan hails from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 20 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 20-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Shrihan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Shrihan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Shrihan saga began at 2023 South CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shrihan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Shrihan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shrihan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ishan Vinothkumar: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ishan Vinothkumar has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Jocelyn Jerome has emotionally recovered.

Shrihan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shrihan has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shrihan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Shrihan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Shrihan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Shrihan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Shrihan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Shrihan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Shrihan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.
