shrihayagrivan_karthigayan.name: Shrihayagrivan Karthigayan
shrihayagrivan_karthigayan.summary:
Out of California, Shrihayagrivan Karthigayan has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in BS U11. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Shrihayagrivan belongs with the best in the country.

What makes Shrihayagrivan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 3 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-3 (BS U11) • 4 ORC titles.

The numbers tell a compelling story: Shrihayagrivan boasts a biggest win margin of 39 points, 67 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a career +1023 point differential.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Shrihayagrivan adapts to anyone:
  • Toby Liu (current primary partner) — 13 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (16 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Shreya Rajesh Kumar (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Kavya Balaji (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 4 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Shrihayagrivan is still ascending.

Shrihayagrivan has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Shrihayagrivan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Shrihayagrivan has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Shrihayagrivan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
shrihayagrivan_karthigayan.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Shrihayagrivan Karthigayan, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-3. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Shrihayagrivan has 45 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 13% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shrihayagrivan saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shrihayagrivan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 18 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shrihayagrivan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Shrihayagrivan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Shrihayagrivan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Toby Liu (16 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BD U11 — we hope Toby Liu has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has seen Shrihayagrivan 5 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Tournament committees have handed Shrihayagrivan a #1 or #2 seed 5 times, and Shrihayagrivan has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships in BS U13, where Shrihayagrivan was seeded #1 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shrihayagrivan is not just losing locally — Shrihayagrivan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Karthigayan family travels cross-country so Shrihayagrivan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shrihayagrivan has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shrihayagrivan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13, which means Shrihayagrivan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Shrihayagrivan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Shrihayagrivan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Shrihayagrivan story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 6 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. But every season Shrihayagrivan comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Shrihayagrivan, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 93 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 43 of them, to be precise. Shrivaishnavi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shrivaishnavi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Sanjanaa V Priya: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Sanjanaa V Priya has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Niranjan Manjith has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Shrivaishnavi entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Shrivaishnavi's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Shrivaishnavi and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Shrivaishnavi has competed 11 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shrivaishnavi is not just losing locally — Shrivaishnavi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jami family travels cross-country so Shrivaishnavi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shrivaishnavi has 55 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shrivaishnavi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Shrivaishnavi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Shrivaishnavi entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Shrivaishnavi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 6 seasons and 93 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Shrivaishnavi is not most humans. Shrivaishnavi is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Shrivaishnavi the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Shrivaishnavi. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

shriya_batra.name: Shriya Batra
shriya_batra.summary:
Representing Virginia, Shriya Batra has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 22 entries in 9 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Shriya has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Shriya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Shriya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Shaurya Batra (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (8 events, spanning 3 seasons)

Shriya has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Shriya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
shriya_batra.roast:
Step right up and meet Shriya Batra, from Virginia, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 22 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 22-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Shriya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Shriya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Shriya saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U11) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shriya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 22 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Shriya has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Shaurya Batra: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Shaurya Batra has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Shaurya Batra has emotionally recovered.

Shriya has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Shriya has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shriya has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shriya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, which means Shriya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Shriya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Shriya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Shriya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Shriya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Shriya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

shriya_chockalingam.name: Shriya Chockalingam
shriya_chockalingam.summary:
Representing California, Shriya Chockalingam is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Shriya in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Shriya competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U19 GD) • Junior Nationals top-4 (GD U19) • 1 ORC title.

The 2021-2022 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Shriya is still ascending.

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, SoCal, Shriya isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Shriya has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Shriya's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
shriya_chockalingam.roast:
Step right up and meet Shriya Chockalingam, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 10 career entries in 6 tournaments, Shriya has 1 title — a 10% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 9 entries where Shriya drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 10 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Shriya saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2021 HPBC NW ORC in GS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Shriya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Shriya would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Shriya has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Shriya has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Shriya's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Shriya entered 2 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Shriya's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Shriya and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Shriya 2 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Shriya has been seeded in 6 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Shriya takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U19, and finishing 13th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Shriya. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Shriya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

The Shriya story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 2 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who treats seedings like suggestions rather than expectations. But every season Shriya comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Shriya, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

shriyans_bhagavatula.name: Shriyans Bhagavatula
shriyans_bhagavatula.summary:
Out of California, Shriyans Bhagavatula has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 XD, U15 BS, U19 BS) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 11 career titles across 20 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Shriyans is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (9 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Shriyans is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 4 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 XD, U15 BS, U19 BS) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BS U13) • 9 ORC titles.

Shriyans has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Brianna Y Lee — 3 events in U17/XD, producing 2 titles together
  • Aagnaey Gulla Pulijal — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Shriyans has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Shriyans has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Shriyans has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience and 11 titles, Shriyans is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
shriyans_bhagavatula.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Shriyans Bhagavatula, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Shriyans is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Shriyans has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Shriyans would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Shriyans, we kept reading — all 54 entries across 20 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 20% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Shriyans held up a trophy, there were roughly 3 other times Shriyans walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Shriyans saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shriyans burst onto the scene with a title in BS U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Shriyans has delivered on some of those promises: 11 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Shriyans promised and what Shriyans has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 4 titles in 7 entries — a stretch where Shriyans actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Shriyans is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Shriyans reaches a final. Shriyans has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shriyans entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Shriyans does own 11 actual titles, so it is not like Shriyans has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 11 wins means Shriyans is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shriyans plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Shriyans can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Shriyans has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shriyans's texts. The go-to partner is Rocky Yang (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 NorCal CRC in XD U15 — we hope Xinyan Zeng has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Shriyans has been seeded in 16 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Shriyans takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Shriyans. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Shriyans of is playing it safe. Shriyans has entered older age groups at 9 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Shriyans has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 9 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Shriyans has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shriyans is not just losing locally — Shriyans is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 20% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Bhagavatula family travels cross-country so Shriyans can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shriyans has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shriyans showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U17, which means Shriyans traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip.

In the end, Shriyans's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 11 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 43 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Shriyans is a player who chokes in finals like it is a paid hobby. Will Shriyans figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Shriyans's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

Shu has 21 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Shu saga began at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Shu plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Shu has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Shu's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U19 — we hope Matthew Lee has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Shu has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Shu has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shu has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shu showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) in BS U17, which means Shu traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Shu entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Shu paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Shu keeps showing up. Also, Shu changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Shuoan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Shuoan is not just losing locally — Shuoan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xiang family travels cross-country so Shuoan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Shuoan has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Shuoan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15, which means Shuoan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Shuoan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Shuoan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Shuoan's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 45 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Shuoan is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Shuoan figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Shuoan's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

siddhangi_wickramasinghe.name: Siddhangi Wickramasinghe
siddhangi_wickramasinghe.summary:
Hailing from New York, Siddhangi Wickramasinghe has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 12 entries in 6 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Notably, Siddhangi has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Siddhangi especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Siddhangi has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Siddhangi's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
siddhangi_wickramasinghe.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Siddhangi Wickramasinghe, from New York, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Siddhangi's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Siddhangi keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Siddhangi saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Siddhangi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Siddhangi has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15 — we hope Yiyang Xu has emotionally recovered.

Siddhangi has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Siddhangi has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siddhangi has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siddhangi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Siddhangi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Siddhangi entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Siddhangi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Siddhangi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Siddhangi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Siddhangi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

siddhant_phatak.name: Siddhant Phatak
siddhant_phatak.summary: Out of Massachusetts, Siddhant Phatak is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Siddhant's.
siddhant_phatak.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Siddhant Phatak, from Massachusetts, continues to exist, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Siddhant is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 NE MassBad OLC, and so far, the only thing Siddhant has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Siddhant saga began at 2025 NE MassBad OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Siddhant is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Siddhant's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Siddhant is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siddhant has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siddhant showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 NE MassBad OLC in BS U17, which means Siddhant traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Siddhant is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Siddhant wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

siddhant_pruthi.name: Siddhant Pruthi
siddhant_pruthi.summary:
Out of California, Siddhant Pruthi has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 2 times. With 21 entries across 8 tournaments, Siddhant is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

A true triple-threat, Siddhant competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Siddhant has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Lucas Lu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Zoe Wong (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Siddhant has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Siddhant has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Siddhant brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
siddhant_pruthi.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Siddhant Pruthi, from California, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Siddhant has 21 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Siddhant saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Can we talk about Siddhant and Boys' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Siddhant can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Siddhant still cannot reach it.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Siddhant's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Siddhant has reached the finals 2 times — at 2026 Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC, 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Siddhant has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Siddhant plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Siddhant has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Siddhant's texts. The go-to partner is Lucas Lu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Julian Saldana Heraud has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Siddhant has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Siddhant is not just losing locally — Siddhant is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Pruthi family travels cross-country so Siddhant can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siddhant has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siddhant showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Siddhant traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Siddhant entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 45th, which is a polite way of saying Siddhant paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Siddhant keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Siddhant has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Siddhant knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Siddhant has turned Boys' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Siddharth is 0-for-12. Zero titles in 12 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Siddharth and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Siddharth needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Siddharth has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Siddharth's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Rianshi Kuchi: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Rianshi Kuchi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Aayush Jaltar has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Siddharth's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Siddharth has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Siddharth has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siddharth has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siddharth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Siddharth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Siddharth entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Siddharth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Siddharth's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 34 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Siddharth is a player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. Will Siddharth figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Siddharth's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

  • Mithun Gopinath — 2 events in BD, best: #2

Siddharth has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Siddharth brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
siddharth_sriganesh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Siddharth Sriganesh, from Georgia, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Siddharth has 19 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Siddharth saga began at 2023 South CRC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Siddharth's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Siddharth has reached the finals 2 times — at 2024 South CRC (TBTT), 2023 South OLC (TBTT) — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Siddharth has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Siddharth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 19 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Siddharth has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Vishanth Vijay (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U15 — we hope Vishanth Vijay has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Siddharth has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Siddharth has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siddharth has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siddharth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Siddharth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Siddharth entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Siddharth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Siddharth keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Siddharth has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Siddharth, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

siddhi_thorat.name: Siddhi Thorat
siddhi_thorat.summary:
Hailing from California, Siddhi Thorat is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Siddhi in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Notably, Siddhi has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Siddhi competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U13 GD) • Junior Nationals top-3 (GD U11, XD U11) • 3 ORC titles.

Diving into the match data reveals: Siddhi boasts a clutch 52% win rate in three-game matches (12/23), 45 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a career +511 point differential.

Siddhi has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 19 different players across the career:
  • Chiranjeev Deepak (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (8 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Rachel Yao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U13 (5 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Aayush Jaladurgam (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #4)
  • Yuki Guan (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Siddhi is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Siddhi has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Siddhi has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Siddhi has been seeded #1 or #2 in 5 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Siddhi is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
siddhi_thorat.roast:
Allow us to introduce Siddhi Thorat, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 64 career entries in 25 tournaments, Siddhi has 3 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 20 entries where Siddhi drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 64 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Siddhi saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Siddhi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Siddhi actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Siddhi is 0-for-16. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Can we talk about Siddhi and Girls' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 1 losses. Siddhi can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Siddhi still cannot reach it. Can we talk about Siddhi and Girls' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 4 finals, 4 losses. Siddhi can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Siddhi still cannot reach it.

If Siddhi's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Siddhi has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 SoCal ORC, where Siddhi entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U11, XD U11, GD U11). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Siddhi does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Siddhi has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 3 wins means Siddhi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Siddhi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Siddhi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Siddhi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Siddhi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Chiranjeev Deepak: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Chiranjeev Deepak has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GD U13 — we hope Sherlyn Mei has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Siddhi entered 3 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Siddhi's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Siddhi and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Siddhi 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Siddhi has been seeded in 15 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Siddhi takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Siddhi. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Siddhi has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Siddhi is not just losing locally — Siddhi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Thorat family travels cross-country so Siddhi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siddhi has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siddhi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Siddhi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Siddhi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Siddhi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles, 64 entries, and 61 that ended without a trophy. A player who has turned Girls' Singles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Siddhi looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Siddhi. We will be watching.

sidhanth_manivannan.name: Sidhanth Manivannan
sidhanth_manivannan.summary: A Georgia competitor, Sidhanth Manivannan is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Sidhanth's.
sidhanth_manivannan.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Sidhanth Manivannan, from Georgia, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Sidhanth is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Fortius South CRC, and so far, the only thing Sidhanth has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Sidhanth saga began at 2025 Fortius South CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Sidhanth is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Sidhanth's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Sidhanth is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sidhanth has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sidhanth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Fortius South CRC in BS U15, which means Sidhanth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Sidhanth is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Sidhanth wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

sidharth_ramachandran.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Sidharth Ramachandran, from Oregon, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Sidharth's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Sidharth keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Sidharth saga began at 2024 OBA NW OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Sidharth has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sidharth has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sidharth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U17, which means Sidharth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Sidharth entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Sidharth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sidharth keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sidharth has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sidharth knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

sienna_lee.name: Sienna Lee
sienna_lee.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Sienna Lee knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 37 entries across 13 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Sienna has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Sienna especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Sienna adapts to anyone:
  • Jida Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Alicia Chang (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #2)

Sienna has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Sienna is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
sienna_lee.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Sienna Lee, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sienna has 37 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sienna saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2025 NE MassBad OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sienna's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 15 entries — a stretch where Sienna actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sienna is 0-for-18. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

In GS U13, Sienna was once THE player to beat — 1 title including at 2025 NE MassBad OLC. Fast forward to now, Sienna is finishing 9th, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Sienna would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Sienna has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Sienna has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Sienna's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sienna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Sienna is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Sienna has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sienna's texts. The go-to partner is Amelia Wang (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13 — we hope Lucas Liu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sienna's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Sienna has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Sienna has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Sienna has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sienna has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sienna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in GS U13, which means Sienna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15), where Sienna entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Sienna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sienna's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 36 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sienna is a player who proof that glory has an expiration date and Sienna's is past due. Will Sienna figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sienna's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.
