With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Siming(Alan) brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
siming_alan_qu.roast:
Step right up and meet Siming(Alan) Qu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Siming(Alan) has 31 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Siming(Alan) saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Siming(Alan) plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 31 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Siming(Alan) has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Evan Lu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Evan Lu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Evan Lu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Siming(Alan) has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Siming(Alan) is not just losing locally — Siming(Alan) is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Qu family travels cross-country so Siming(Alan) can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siming(Alan) has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siming(Alan) showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Siming(Alan) traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Siming(Alan) entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Siming(Alan) paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 31 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Siming(Alan) is not most humans. Siming(Alan) is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Siming(Alan) the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Siming(Alan). The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

It all started at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC, and so far, the only thing Sing has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Sing saga began at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sing plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Sing has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Xinyang Peng has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sing has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sing showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U17, which means Sing traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Sing entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Sing paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sing keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sing has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sing knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

sitan_zhan.name: Sitan Zhan
sitan_zhan.summary:
Out of Massachusetts, Sitan Zhan has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Sitan's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Sitan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-4 finisher.
sitan_zhan.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Sitan Zhan, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 2 wins across 9 entries in 3 tournaments. That is a 22% conversion rate, which means roughly 8 out of every 10 times Sitan shows up to a tournament, Sitan pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Sitan saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 NE CRC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sitan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sitan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sitan is 0-for-3. Zero titles in 3 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sitan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sitan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Sitan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.'

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sitan's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Sitan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sitan has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sitan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 South Frisco ORC in BS U17, which means Sitan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Sitan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Sitan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

sitar_nainish_sanghani.name: Sitar Nainish Sanghani
sitar_nainish_sanghani.summary:
Hailing from Pennsylvania, Sitar Nainish Sanghani has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Sitar's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Sitar has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Sitar competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (6 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Sitar has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Zisheng (GaoGao) Fan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (8 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

Sitar has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Sitar has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Sitar brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sitar_nainish_sanghani.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Sitar Nainish Sanghani, from Pennsylvania, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 27 career entries in 10 tournaments, Sitar has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 26 entries where Sitar drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 27 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sitar saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sitar plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Sitar has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sitar's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Zisheng (GaoGao) Fan: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Zisheng (GaoGao) Fan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in GD U13 — we hope Haziqa H Mathani has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sitar entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sitar's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sitar and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Sitar has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Sitar has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sitar is not just losing locally — Sitar is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sanghani family travels cross-country so Sitar can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sitar has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sitar showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U13, which means Sitar traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Sitar entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Sitar paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Sitar keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sitar has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sitar, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

sitar_sanghani.name: Sitar Sanghani

The numbers tell a compelling story: Sitar boasts 34 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a positive +410 point differential across the career, a solid 67% win rate over 67 matches.

  • Chingpei Lin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Iris Kuan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (5 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Aditya Namboodiripad (current partner) — 9 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons
  • Adhip Sundar (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 3 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Sitar is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Sitar has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Sitar has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 5 titles, Sitar has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
sitar_sanghani.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Sitar Sanghani file just landed on our desk. Sitar hails from Pennsylvania, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sitar has 58 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 5 of them — a 9% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sitar saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sitar's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 14 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Can we talk about Sitar and Girls' Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 3 finals, 2 losses. Sitar can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Sitar still cannot reach it. In Girls' Singles, Sitar has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sitar chokes in Girls' Singles finals specifically.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Sitar reaches a final. Sitar has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 NE OLC (NVBC), where Sitar entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sitar does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Sitar has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 5 wins means Sitar is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sitar plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sitar can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Sitar has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sitar's texts. The go-to partner is Aditya Namboodiripad (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Jonathan Wu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sitar's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Sitar has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Then there is the seeding situation. Sitar has been seeded in 13 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Sitar takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 Capital NE OLC in GS U15, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Sitar. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Sitar has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sitar is not just losing locally — Sitar is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sanghani family travels cross-country so Sitar can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sitar has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sitar showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Sitar traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Sitar entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 26th, which is a polite way of saying Sitar paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Sitar story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 5 titles across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who has turned Girls' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. But every season Sitar comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Sitar, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

siva_kondagunta.name: Siva Kondagunta
siva_kondagunta.summary: A North Carolina competitor, Siva Kondagunta is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Siva's.
siva_kondagunta.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Siva Kondagunta file just landed on our desk. Siva hails from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Siva is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 South OLC (TBTT), and so far, the only thing Siva has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Siva saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Siva is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Siva's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Siva is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Siva is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Siva wants to be. siva_rajesh.name: Siva Rajesh
siva_rajesh.summary:
Out of North Carolina, Siva Rajesh has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Siva's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Siva has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Siva competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior Championships competitor (2025) • U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Siva has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 18 different players across the career:
  • Alan Premkumar — a trusted BD/U19 partner (5 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

Siva has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Siva has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Siva consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Siva has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
siva_rajesh.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Siva Rajesh, from North Carolina, continues to exist, currently competing at the U19 level. Hold on — Siva has competed at the Pan American Junior Championships. That is an international tournament where you literally represent your country. Sounds amazing, right? Except Siva finished with a best of top-9, which means Siva flew to another country, wore the red-white-and-blue, stepped on court as a representative of the United States... and got bounced before the podium was even set up. Patriotism is beautiful. The results were not. Moving on to the other 51 career entries.

The Siva saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2023 South CRC in BS U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Siva's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 23 entries — a stretch where Siva actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Siva is 0-for-1. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Here is a fun detail: in BS U19, Siva used to be seeded #1 (at 2023 South CRC). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2023 Midwest ORC). Tournament committees are literally telling Siva 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Siva plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Siva is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Siva has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Siva's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Alan Premkumar: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alan Premkumar has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Rishita Tubati has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Siva's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Siva entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Siva's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Siva and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Siva has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Siva has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Siva is not just losing locally — Siva is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Rajesh family travels cross-country so Siva can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siva has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siva showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Siva traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Siva entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Siva paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title, 52 entries, and 51 that ended without a trophy. A player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Siva looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Siva. We will be watching.

siya_alicia_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Siya Alicia Chen, from Georgia, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Siya has 48 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 12% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Siya saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2024 South CRC (TBTT) in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Siya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 4 titles in 25 entries — a stretch where Siya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 8 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Siya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Siya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Siya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Siya's texts. The go-to partner is Vishanth Vijay (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Jasmine Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Siya's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Siya has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Siya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Siya has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Siya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Siya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Siya entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Siya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Siya story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 6 titles across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. But every season Siya comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Siya, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

skylar_zhen_yu_tan.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Skylar Zhen Yu Tan, from Texas, continues to exist, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 13 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 13-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Skylar's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Skylar keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Skylar saga began at 2022 South Frisco U11 OLC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Skylar plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Skylar has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Ziming Zhao has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Skylar has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Skylar showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13, which means Skylar traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Skylar entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Skylar paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

4 seasons. 13 entries. Zero titles. If this were a stock, the SEC would have delisted it by now. If this were a restaurant, the health inspector would have questions. If this were a dating profile, the app would gently suggest 'trying a different approach.' But this is junior badminton, where hope springs eternal and so does Skylar's entry form. We salute the commitment. We question the strategy. We will absolutely be back next season to see if anything changes. (It will not.)

soham_kulkarni.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Soham Kulkarni, from Washington, continues to exist, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 win across 11 entries in 6 tournaments. That is a 9% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Soham shows up to a tournament, Soham pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Soham saga began at 2024 OBA NW OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2026 Seattle NW CRC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Soham's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Soham's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Soham has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Soham showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U15, which means Soham traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Soham entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Soham paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title, 11 entries, and 10 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Soham looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Soham. We will be watching.

sohum_gulla_pulijal.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Sohum Gulla Pulijal, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 25 career entries in 10 tournaments, Sohum has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 24 entries where Sohum drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 25 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sohum saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sohum plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 25 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Sohum has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sohum's texts. The go-to partner is Anusha Jamsandekar (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U19 — we hope Raghava Krishna Katakam has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sohum entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sohum's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sohum and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Sohum has shown up 5 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Sohum has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sohum is not just losing locally — Sohum is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Pulijal family travels cross-country so Sohum can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sohum has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sohum showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Sohum traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Sohum entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Sohum paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Sohum keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sohum has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sohum, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

sophia_coco_wang.name: Sophia Coco Wang
sophia_coco_wang.summary:
Hailing from California, Sophia Coco Wang is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Sophia is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Sophia especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Toby Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Having moved up to U15, Sophia is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Sophia brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Sophia has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Sophia has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Sophia has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
sophia_coco_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Sophia Coco Wang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 42 career entries in 14 tournaments, Sophia has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 41 entries where Sophia drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 42 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sophia saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sophia's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 15 entries — a stretch where Sophia actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sophia is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Sophia's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Sophia has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Sophia entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U11, XD U11, GD U11). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sophia does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Sophia has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Sophia is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sophia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sophia is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sophia and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sophia needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Sophia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sophia's texts. The go-to partner is Vignan Chandrasekaran (4 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Surya Peechara has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sophia entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Sophia's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sophia and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Sophia has shown up 6 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

One thing nobody can accuse Sophia of is playing it safe. Sophia has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Sophia has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Sophia has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sophia is not just losing locally — Sophia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wang family travels cross-country so Sophia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sophia has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sophia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13, which means Sophia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event in U13 XD. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Sophia entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Sophia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title, 42 entries, and 41 that ended without a trophy. A player who treats championship matches like optional practice sessions. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Sophia looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Sophia. We will be watching.

The Sophia saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. Best finish: 2nd. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left. In GD U17, Sophia was once THE player to beat — 2 titles including at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. These days, Sophia is finishing 3rd, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date.

Mixed Doubles finals and Sophia — name a more iconic tragedy. 7 trips to the championship match, and Sophia has turned 2 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Sophia's trophies to save time.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Sophia experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Sophia has reached 18 championship matches across this career. That is 18 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 18 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Pan Am Junior, where Sophia entered 5 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (GS U17, XD U17, XD U17). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sophia does own 19 actual titles, so it is not like Sophia has never won anything. But 18 finals losses versus 19 wins means Sophia is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 6 singles, 5 doubles, 5 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sophia can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Sophia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sophia's texts. The most successful partnership is with Arush Shyam Chitgopkar — 17 events, 6 titles — and honestly, Arush Shyam Chitgopkar deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Arush Shyam Chitgopkar a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13 — we hope Prajwal Avadhani has emotionally recovered.

Sophia has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sophia is not just losing locally — Sophia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 25% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Jia family travels cross-country so Sophia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U13, which means Sophia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Sophia entered 3 events with results ranging from 2nd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Sophia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sophia's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 19 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 57 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sophia is a player who living proof that past performance does not guarantee future results. Will Sophia figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sophia's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

Sophia can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Girls' Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Sophia still cannot reach it.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Sophia's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Sophia has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Sophia does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Sophia has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 5 wins means Sophia is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sophia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sophia can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 24 of them, to be precise. Sophia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sophia's texts. The go-to partner is Prathik Nemani (11 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GD U15 — we hope Sophia Sun has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sophia entered 6 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sophia's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sophia and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Sophia 9 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

One thing nobody can accuse Sophia of is playing it safe. Sophia has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Sophia has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Sophia has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sophia is not just losing locally — Sophia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Sophia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sophia has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sophia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Sophia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Sophia entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Sophia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sophia's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 5 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 78 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sophia is a player who has turned Girls' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. Will Sophia figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sophia's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sophia_lin.name: Sophia Lin
sophia_lin.summary:
Representing Illinois, Sophia Lin is a serial champion on the local circuit, currently competing at the U17 level, racking up 6 titles across 16 tournaments. That winning pedigree at the local and club level creates the perfect launchpad for success at the regional and national stages.

Sophia is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Sophia especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 4 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Sophia adapts to anyone:
  • Matthew H Ponta (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Charlotte Richards (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in GD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Tiffany C Sun — 3 events in GD, best: #2, across 2 seasons

Sophia is peaking at the right time in 2024-2025, with 3 titles across 3 tournaments — a clear sign that Sophia is still ascending.

Sophia has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Sophia has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Sophia is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
sophia_lin.roast:
Step right up and meet Sophia Lin, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sophia has 46 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 13% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sophia saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2022 Egret Midwest CRC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sophia's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2024-2025 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 9 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

XD U17 used to be Sophia's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2024 MBC Midwest OLC era. Now Sophia is grinding for a top-5 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away. GD U17 used to be Sophia's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2024 MBC Midwest OLC era. Now Sophia is grinding for a top-17 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sophia chokes in Girls' Singles finals specifically.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Sophia reaches a final. Sophia has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Sophia does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Sophia has never won anything. Sophia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sophia is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sophia and suddenly the wins start appearing — 6 titles in doubles and mixed. Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sophia's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Sophia has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

One thing nobody can accuse Sophia of is playing it safe. Sophia has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Sophia has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 6 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Sophia has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sophia is not just losing locally — Sophia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 13% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lin family travels cross-country so Sophia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sophia has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sophia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in XD U15, which means Sophia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Sophia entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Sophia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles, 46 entries, and 40 that ended without a trophy. A player who living proof that past performance does not guarantee future results. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Sophia looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Sophia. We will be watching.
