So buckle in, because we have 81 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sophia saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC, where Sophia burst onto the scene with a title in GS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Sophia has delivered on some of those promises: 10 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Sophia promised and what Sophia has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 5 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Sophia actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

In Girls' Doubles, Sophia has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 4 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sophia chokes in Girls' Doubles finals specifically.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Sophia reaches a final. Sophia has reached 11 championship matches across this career. That is 11 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 11 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NorCal CRC, where Sophia entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sophia does own 10 actual titles, so it is not like Sophia has never won anything. But 11 finals losses versus 10 wins means Sophia is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sophia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 5 singles, 3 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sophia can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Sophia has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sophia's texts. The most successful partnership is with Alice Meng — 16 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Alice Meng deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Alice Meng a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GD U15 — we hope Sophia Li has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sophia entered 9 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sophia's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sophia and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Sophia has been seeded in 24 events and underperformed the seeding 7 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Sophia takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U13, and finishing 33rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Sophia. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Sophia of is playing it safe. Sophia has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Sophia has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 4 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Sophia has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sophia is not just losing locally — Sophia is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sun family travels cross-country so Sophia can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sophia has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sophia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U13, which means Sophia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Sophia entered 3 events with results ranging from 1st to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Sophia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sophia's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 10 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 71 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sophia is a player who has turned Girls' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. Will Sophia figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sophia's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in GS U13, which means Soudamini traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Soudamini entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 3 titles, 48 entries, and 45 that ended without a trophy. A player who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Soudamini looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Soudamini. We will be watching.

soumith_parasa.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Soumith Parasa, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 20 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 20-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Soumith's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Soumith keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Soumith saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Soumith plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Soumith has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Soumith's texts. The go-to partner is Anirudh Krishna Balaganesh (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Vidhu Reddy Keesara has emotionally recovered.

Soumith has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Soumith has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Soumith has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Soumith showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Soumith traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Soumith entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Soumith paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Soumith keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Soumith has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Soumith knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Soumith changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

sourish_yanamandra.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Sourish Yanamandra, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sourish has 52 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sourish saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Mixed Doubles finals and Sourish — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Sourish has turned 2 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Sourish's trophies to save time.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Sourish's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Sourish has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2026 Wayside NE CRC, where Sourish entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. And remember: Sourish has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sourish plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 52 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Sourish has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sourish's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Lili Yuan Li: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Lili Yuan Li has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Disha Vijayakumar has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Sourish has shown up 3 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Sourish has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sourish is not just losing locally — Sourish is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yanamandra family travels cross-country so Sourish can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sourish has 34 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sourish showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Sourish traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Sourish entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Sourish paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 4 seasons and 52 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Sourish is not most humans. Sourish is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Sourish the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Sourish. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

sreeskandan_saravanan.name: Sreeskandan Saravanan
sreeskandan_saravanan.summary:
A Massachusetts competitor, Sreeskandan Saravanan has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U11 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Sreeskandan's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Sreeskandan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Sreeskandan adapts to anyone:
  • Manvi Jandhyala (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events)
  • Tailong Cheng (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2)
  • Virat Satish (current primary partner) — 3 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (3 total events in BD)
  • Akilan Selvamanickam (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2023-2024 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 8 tournaments. Sreeskandan is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Sreeskandan has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 6 seeded entries across the career, Sreeskandan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Sreeskandan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
sreeskandan_saravanan.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Sreeskandan Saravanan file just landed on our desk. Sreeskandan hails from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sreeskandan has 31 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sreeskandan saga began at 2023 MassBad NE OLC, where Sreeskandan burst onto the scene with a title in XD U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Sreeskandan has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Sreeskandan promised and what Sreeskandan has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

In Boys' Doubles, Sreeskandan has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sreeskandan chokes in Boys' Doubles finals specifically. In Boys' Singles, Sreeskandan has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 2 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Sreeskandan chokes in Boys' Singles finals specifically.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Sreeskandan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Sreeskandan has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sreeskandan does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Sreeskandan has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 2 wins means Sreeskandan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sreeskandan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sreeskandan is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sreeskandan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sreeskandan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Sreeskandan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sreeskandan's texts. The go-to partner is Manvi Jandhyala (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U11) in BD U11 — we hope Tailong Cheng has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sreeskandan's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Sreeskandan has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Tournament committees have handed Sreeskandan a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Sreeskandan has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 NE MassBad OLC in BS U11, where Sreeskandan was seeded #1 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sreeskandan is not just losing locally — Sreeskandan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Saravanan family travels cross-country so Sreeskandan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sreeskandan has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sreeskandan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U11, which means Sreeskandan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 NE ORC (U11) in BD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Sreeskandan entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Sreeskandan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sreeskandan's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 29 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Sreeskandan is a player who has turned Boys' Doubles finals into a personal art form of almost-winning. Will Sreeskandan figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sreeskandan's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sri_nidhi_vasamsetti.roast:
Step right up and meet Sri Nidhi Vasamsetti, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 23 events across 11 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 23-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Sri's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Sri keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Sri saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sri plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 23 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Sri has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sri's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Emma Qin: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Emma Qin has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Justin Liu has emotionally recovered.

Sri has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sri has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sri showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Sri traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Sri entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Sri paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

After 5 seasons and 23 entries without a title, most reasonable humans would consider the data, accept the hypothesis, and pivot to pickleball. But Sri is not most humans. Sri is a force of nature — a badminton Sisyphus, rolling the shuttle uphill one tournament at a time, watching it roll back down, and showing up next weekend to do it again. The racket bag is always packed. The entry form is always submitted. The result is always the same. And somehow, that makes Sri the most interesting player on this list. Keep going, Sri. The sport is better with you in it — and the roasts are certainly better.

So from the bottom of our hearts, Sri: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Sriaa entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Sriaa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sriaa keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sriaa has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sriaa knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Sriaa changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

srihari_shyam.name: Srihari Shyam
srihari_shyam.summary:
Out of North Carolina, Srihari Shyam is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (BD U11) puts Srihari in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Notably, Srihari has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Srihari competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (BD U11) • ORC top-2 finisher.

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Srihari adapts to anyone:
  • Kavya Balaji (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (8 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Virat Satish (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #2)

Srihari is peaking at the right time in 2024-2025, with 2 titles across 9 tournaments — a clear sign that Srihari is still ascending.

Srihari has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Srihari has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Srihari consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Srihari brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
srihari_shyam.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Srihari Shyam, from North Carolina, continues to exist, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Srihari has 39 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Srihari saga began at 2023 South CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 NE MassBad OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Srihari's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2024-2025 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 26 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Remember when Srihari used to win BS U11? Because Srihari sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Srihari took gold at 2025 NE MassBad OLC, and since then? Best finish: 5th. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left.

In Boys' Doubles, Srihari has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 2 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Srihari chokes in Boys' Doubles finals specifically.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Srihari reaches a final. Srihari has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Srihari does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Srihari has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 2 wins means Srihari is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Srihari plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Srihari can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Srihari has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Srihari's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Kavya Balaji: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Kavya Balaji has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BD U11 — we hope Virat Satish has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Srihari's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Srihari has competed 3 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Srihari has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Srihari is not just losing locally — Srihari is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shyam family travels cross-country so Srihari can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Srihari has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Srihari showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U11, which means Srihari traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, where Srihari entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Srihari paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Srihari story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who proof that glory has an expiration date and Srihari's is past due. But every season Srihari comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Srihari, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Srikrishna plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Srikrishna has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Srikrishna's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Serena Yang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Serena Yang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Aryan R Cimala has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Srikrishna has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Srikrishna is not just losing locally — Srikrishna is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sriganesh family travels cross-country so Srikrishna can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Srikrishna has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Srikrishna showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Srikrishna traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NW ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Srikrishna entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Srikrishna paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Srikrishna keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Srikrishna has survived 3 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Srikrishna knows that better than anyone — but something. Also, Srikrishna changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases, which we felt needed mentioning one more time.

srineet_atluri.name: Srineet Atluri
srineet_atluri.summary:
Representing North Carolina, Srineet Atluri is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-3 finish at Selection places Srineet in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Srineet is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Srineet is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 7 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U13 BD) • Junior Nationals top-2 (XD U13, BS U13, BD U15, BD U13) • 11 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Srineet boasts 53 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a career +831 point differential, a clutch 71% win rate in three-game matches (20/28).

Srineet has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 19 different players across the career:
  • Stephanie Jiayi Song (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (21 total events in U13/U15/XD, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Mikael Chang (current primary partner) — 9 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (15 total events in BD/U15, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Jacob Ma (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 4 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Srineet is still ascending.

Srineet has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Srineet has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Srineet has been seeded #1 or #2 in 14 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 14 titles, Srineet has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
srineet_atluri.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Srineet Atluri, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 95 career entries in 35 tournaments, Srineet has 14 titles — a 15% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 5 entries where Srineet drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 95 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Srineet saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 1 entries and a best finish of 6th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Frisco ORC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Srineet's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

In Boys' Singles, Srineet has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 6 championship appearances, 4 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Srineet chokes in Boys' Singles finals specifically. Boys' Doubles finals and Srineet — name a more iconic tragedy. 9 trips to the championship match, and Srineet has turned 4 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Srineet's trophies to save time.

The seeding trajectory in BS U15 tells a story: #1 at 2023 South CRC, down to #2 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. When the people whose job is to rank you start ranking you lower, that is not a slump — that is a trend line with a very clear direction, and it is not pointing up.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Srineet reaches a final. Srineet has reached 20 championship matches across this career. That is 20 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 20 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Srineet entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U11, XD U11, BD U11). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (6 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (14 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Srineet does own 14 actual titles, so it is not like Srineet has never won anything. But 20 finals losses versus 14 wins means Srineet is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Srineet plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 5 doubles, 7 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Srineet can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Srineet has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Srineet's texts. The most successful partnership is with Stephanie Jiayi Song — 21 events, 5 titles — and honestly, Stephanie Jiayi Song deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Stephanie Jiayi Song a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15 — we hope Stephanie Jiayi Song has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Srineet entered 9 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Srineet's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Srineet and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 10 entries, 3 finals appearances, and zero titles. Srineet has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 3 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell. Then there is the seeding situation. Srineet has been seeded in 28 events and underperformed the seeding 10 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Srineet takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U13, and finishing 33rd. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Srineet. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Srineet of is playing it safe. Srineet has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Srineet has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 10 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Srineet has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Srineet is not just losing locally — Srineet is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Atluri family travels cross-country so Srineet can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Srineet has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Srineet showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Srineet traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Srineet entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Srineet paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Srineet's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 14 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 81 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Srineet is a player who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Singles. Will Srineet figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Srineet's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Yuancheng Li has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Srinika's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Srinika has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Srinika has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Srinika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Srinika entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Srinika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Srinika story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. But every season Srinika comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Srinika, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Srinivas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Srinivas's texts. The most successful partnership is with Clarinda Jin — 17 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Clarinda Jin deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Clarinda Jin a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Brendan E Chen has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has seen Srinivas 6 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

One thing nobody can accuse Srinivas of is playing it safe. Srinivas has entered older age groups at 9 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Srinivas has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 15 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Srinivas has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Srinivas is not just losing locally — Srinivas is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 26% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Subash family travels cross-country so Srinivas can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Srinivas has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Srinivas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Srinivas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Srinivas entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Srinivas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Srinivas's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 21 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 60 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Srinivas is a player who has a finals record that should come with a trigger warning. Will Srinivas figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Srinivas's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

srinivas_shankar.name: Srinivas Shankar
srinivas_shankar.summary:
Out of Texas, Srinivas Shankar has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Srinivas's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Srinivas has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Srinivas especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Srinivas has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Daksh Thakur (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (7 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Hasini Mateti (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 7 tournaments — a clear sign that Srinivas is still ascending.

Srinivas has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Srinivas has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Srinivas consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Srinivas brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
srinivas_shankar.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Srinivas Shankar, from Texas, continues to exist, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Srinivas has 36 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Srinivas saga began at 2024 DFW South OLC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Peak Sports South OLC (Dec) in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Srinivas's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Here is a fun detail: in BS U11, Srinivas used to be seeded #1 (at 2025 Egret Midwest OLC). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2025 Peak Sports South OLC (Dec)). Tournament committees are literally telling Srinivas 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Srinivas would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Srinivas has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Srinivas has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Srinivas's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Srinivas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Srinivas is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Srinivas and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Srinivas needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Srinivas has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Srinivas's texts. The go-to partner is Daksh Thakur (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Ariel An has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Srinivas's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Srinivas has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Srinivas has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Srinivas is not just losing locally — Srinivas is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shankar family travels cross-country so Srinivas can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Srinivas has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Srinivas showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U11, which means Srinivas traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Srinivas entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 25th. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Srinivas paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Srinivas story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Srinivas comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Srinivas, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

srinivas_subash.name: Srinivas Subash
srinivas_subash.summary:
Representing Illinois, Srinivas Subash is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (BS U17). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Srinivas in rarified international company.

A true triple-threat, Srinivas competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (BS U17, 2025) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Srinivas has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
srinivas_subash.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Srinivas Subash, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Srinivas is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (BS U17), which means Srinivas has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Srinivas's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Srinivas went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Srinivas represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 4 career entries and a 0% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 4 entries worth of material.

The Srinivas saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Srinivas plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 4 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Season after season, Srinivas keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Srinivas has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Srinivas, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

srinivasa_pothu.name: Srinivasa Pothu
srinivasa_pothu.summary: Srinivasa Pothu is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Srinivasa's.
srinivasa_pothu.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Srinivasa Pothu, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Srinivasa is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 NW OLC, and so far, the only thing Srinivasa has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Srinivasa saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Srinivasa is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Srinivasa's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Srinivasa is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Srinivasa is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Srinivasa wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

The Sriyaansh saga began at 2026 Seattle NW CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Sriyaansh is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Sriyaansh's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Sriyaansh is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. With only 1 entries on the books, Sriyaansh is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time.
