sriyansh_chigullapally.name: Sriyansh Chigullapally
sriyansh_chigullapally.summary:
Hailing from California, Sriyansh Chigullapally has tasted victory multiple times, currently competing at the U15 level, capturing 2 titles across 14 tournaments and 38 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Sriyansh keeps finding ways to come out on top.

What makes Sriyansh especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (1 entry) • Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Sriyansh has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Logan Lu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #5)
  • Sanjanaa V Priya (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #2)
  • Kayden Hossain (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Sriyansh has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Sriyansh has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Sriyansh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sriyansh_chigullapally.roast:
Allow us to introduce Sriyansh Chigullapally, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 38 career entries in 14 tournaments, Sriyansh has 2 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Sriyansh drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 38 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sriyansh saga began at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals with 1 entries and a best finish of 65th. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BD U15 - Position 5-8, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sriyansh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sriyansh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sriyansh is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sriyansh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sriyansh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Sriyansh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sriyansh's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Logan Lu: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Logan Lu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Sankeerthi Marasani has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sriyansh's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Sriyansh has shown up 4 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sriyansh is not just losing locally — Sriyansh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chigullapally family travels cross-country so Sriyansh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sriyansh has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sriyansh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Sriyansh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Sriyansh entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Sriyansh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles, 38 entries, and 36 that ended without a trophy. A player who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the moments of brilliance — Sriyansh looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Sriyansh. We will be watching.

Over 4 seasons and 5 titles, Stanley has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
stanley_xing.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Stanley Xing, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Stanley is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (XD U19, BD U19), which means Stanley has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Stanley's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Stanley went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Stanley represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 71 career entries and a 7% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 71 entries worth of material.

The Stanley saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 NW Oregon OLC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Stanley's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2024-2025 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 14 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

BD U19 used to be Stanley's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai era. Now Stanley is grinding for a top-2 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

Boys' Doubles finals and Stanley — name a more iconic tragedy. 5 trips to the championship match, and Stanley has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Stanley's trophies to save time.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Stanley's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Stanley has reached 7 championship matches across this career. That is 7 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 7 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Pan Am Junior, where Stanley entered 5 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BD U19, BD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Stanley does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Stanley has never won anything. But 7 finals losses versus 5 wins means Stanley is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Stanley plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Stanley is 0-for-15. Zero titles in 15 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Stanley and suddenly the wins start appearing — 5 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Stanley needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 25 of them, to be precise. Stanley has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Stanley's texts. The most successful partnership is with Arden Q Lee — 9 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Arden Q Lee deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Arden Q Lee a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Muyang Li has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Stanley entered 6 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Stanley's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Stanley and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Stanley has competed 5 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Stanley is not just losing locally — Stanley is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xing family travels cross-country so Stanley can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Stanley has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Stanley showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Stanley traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Stanley entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Stanley paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Stanley story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 5 titles across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who living proof that past performance does not guarantee future results. But every season Stanley comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Stanley, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Notably, Stella has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Stella especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (GD U19, 2024) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 GD) • Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • 4 ORC titles.

  • Mikayla Cruz (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17/U19 (5 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)

Stella has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Stella has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Stella is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.

stephanie_song.roast:
Step right up and meet Stephanie Song, from Washington, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 2 wins across 5 entries in 2 tournaments. That is a 40% conversion rate, which means roughly 6 out of every 10 times Stephanie shows up to a tournament, Stephanie pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Stephanie saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC, where Stephanie burst onto the scene with a title in GS U11 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Stephanie has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Stephanie promised and what Stephanie has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 2 entries — a stretch where Stephanie actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Stephanie is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Stephanie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Stephanie can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Stephanie's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

In the end, Stephanie's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 3 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. Will Stephanie figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Stephanie's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

stephen_huang.name: Stephen Huang
stephen_huang.summary:
Representing Washington, Stephen Huang has collected 2 tournament titles, currently competing at the U15 level, capturing 2 titles across 10 tournaments and 25 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Stephen keeps finding ways to come out on top.

Stephen is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries).

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Stephen adapts to anyone:
  • Andy Starostin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Elena Zhong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Ryan Chu (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Stephen has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, SoCal, Stephen isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Stephen brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
stephen_huang.roast:
Step right up and meet Stephen Huang, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Stephen has 25 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Stephen saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 2 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2024 SBC NW CRC in XD U13 - Position 5-8, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Stephen's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Stephen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Stephen is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Stephen and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Stephen needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Stephen has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Stephen's texts. The go-to partner is Sean Yang (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Sean Yang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Stephen's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Stephen has shown up 5 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Stephen has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Stephen has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Stephen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Stephen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Stephen entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Stephen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Stephen story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 2 titles across 3 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Stephen comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Stephen, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

stephen_yuan.name: Stephen Yuan
stephen_yuan.summary:
Out of New York, Stephen Yuan is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U15 level, with 6 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Stephen especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
stephen_yuan.roast:
Breaking news from the junior badminton world: Stephen Yuan, from New York, continues to exist, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Stephen is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Stephen has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Stephen saga began at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Stephen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 6 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Stephen has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Stephen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U15, which means Stephen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Stephen entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Stephen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Stephen keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Stephen has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Stephen, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

steve_zhang.name: Steve Zhang
steve_zhang.summary:
Hailing from Oregon, Steve Zhang has stepped onto the competitive stage with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Steve's.

Notably, Steve has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Steve competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Steve has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With only 2 entries on the books, Steve is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Steve. We will have more material. We always do.

steven_lei.name: Steven Lei
steven_lei.summary:
Out of Illinois, Steven Lei has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Steven's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Steven is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Steven especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 23 different doubles partners over the years, Steven adapts to anyone:
  • Hanyi (Sissi) Lei (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Brighton Yu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Allison Zhao (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Tanay Kankanala (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)

Steven is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 3 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Steven is still ascending.

Steven has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Steven has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Steven consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 7 titles, Steven is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
steven_lei.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Steven Lei, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Steven has 71 career entries across 25 tournaments and has managed to win 7 of them — a 10% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Steven saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 Egret Midwest CRC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Steven's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 3 titles in 11 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

BD U15 used to be Steven's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2025 Egret MW CRC era. Now Steven is grinding for a top-9 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

In Boys' Doubles, Steven has a unique gift: the ability to win every match that does NOT matter and lose the one that does. 3 championship appearances, 1 second-place finishes. Scientists should study this — it is statistically remarkable how consistently Steven chokes in Boys' Doubles finals specifically.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Steven reaches a final. Steven has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Steven does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Steven has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 7 wins means Steven is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Steven plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Steven can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Steven has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Steven's texts. The go-to partner is Elena Zhang (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Mithra Senthil Raja has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Steven's 7 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Steven has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Tournament committees have handed Steven a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Steven has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2025 Egret MW CRC in BS U15, where Steven was seeded #2 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Steven of is playing it safe. Steven has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Steven has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Steven has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Steven is not just losing locally — Steven is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lei family travels cross-country so Steven can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Steven has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Steven showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Steven traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Steven entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Steven paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Steven story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 7 titles across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who living proof that past performance does not guarantee future results. But every season Steven comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Steven, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

suhasram_y.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Suhasram Y, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Suhasram's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Suhasram keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Suhasram saga began at 2024 NE NJBC CRC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Suhasram plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Suhasram has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Suhasram has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Suhasram showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13, which means Suhasram traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Suhasram entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Suhasram paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Suhasram keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Suhasram has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Suhasram knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Sumaswara's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Sumaswara competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (1 entry) • Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Sumaswara has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Sumaswara brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sumaswara_chinthalapati.roast:
Allow us to introduce Sumaswara Chinthalapati, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 12 career entries in 8 tournaments, Sumaswara has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Sumaswara drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 12 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sumaswara saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 7th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

At Junior Nationals, Sumaswara has shown up 2 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sumaswara is not just losing locally — Sumaswara is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chinthalapati family travels cross-country so Sumaswara can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sumaswara has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sumaswara showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U19, which means Sumaswara traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

Season after season, Sumaswara keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sumaswara has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sumaswara, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

Sumedh has competed across 4 different regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Sumedh's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
sumedh_madhusudan_pai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Sumedh Madhusudan Pai, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Sumedh has 15 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Sumedh saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sumedh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Sumedh has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Nishaant Anumolu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Shreyas Reddy has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Sumedh has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sumedh is not just losing locally — Sumedh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Pai family travels cross-country so Sumedh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sumedh has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sumedh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13, which means Sumedh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Sumedh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Sumedh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Look, we have been harsh. That is the job. But here is the truth buried under all the sarcasm: Sumedh keeps showing up. In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Sumedh has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Sumedh knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

suneri_krishna_chinthalapati.name: Suneri Krishna Chinthalapati
suneri_krishna_chinthalapati.summary:
A California competitor, Suneri Krishna Chinthalapati has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-4 in GS U19, GD U15. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Suneri belongs with the best in the country.

Suneri is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (8 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Suneri especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals top-4 (GS U19, GD U15) • 3 ORC titles.

Suneri has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Anvita Jamsandekar (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (8 events, best: #2, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Nihal Kadamba — a trusted U15/XD partner (6 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Hannah George — a trusted GD/U17 partner (6 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)

Suneri has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Suneri has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Suneri has been seeded #1 or #2 in 9 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 3 titles, Suneri has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
suneri_krishna_chinthalapati.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Suneri Krishna Chinthalapati file just landed on our desk. Suneri hails from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 59 career entries in 25 tournaments, Suneri has 3 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Suneri drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 59 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Suneri saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 NorCal ORC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Suneri's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Suneri actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Suneri is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

GS U19 used to be Suneri's personal ATM — just show up and collect the gold. That was the 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai era. Now Suneri is grinding for a top-3 finish in the same event. The shuttle giveth, and the shuttle taketh away.

Girls' Doubles finals and Suneri — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Suneri has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Suneri's trophies to save time.

Here is a fun detail: in GS U15, Suneri used to be seeded #1 (at 2022 South Frisco ORC). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC). Tournament committees are literally telling Suneri 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

If Suneri's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Suneri has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Suneri does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Suneri has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 3 wins means Suneri is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Suneri plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Suneri can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Suneri has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Suneri's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Anvita Jamsandekar: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Anvita Jamsandekar has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Suraj Rajesh Kumar has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Suneri entered 11 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Suneri's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Suneri and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Suneri 10 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Suneri has been seeded in 16 events and underperformed the seeding 9 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Suneri takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event in U19 GS, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Suneri. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Suneri of is playing it safe. Suneri has entered older age groups at 8 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Suneri has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Suneri has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Suneri is not just losing locally — Suneri is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chinthalapati family travels cross-country so Suneri can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Suneri has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Suneri showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U17, which means Suneri traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 NorCal CRC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event, where Suneri entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Suneri paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Suneri's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 56 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Suneri is a player who living proof that past performance does not guarantee future results. Will Suneri figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Suneri's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

The first title would not come until 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Sunny's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sunny plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Sunny is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 9th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sunny and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sunny needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Sunny has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sunny's texts. The go-to partner is Bavish Reddi Dornala (4 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ashni Mathure has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sunny's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Sunny has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Sunny has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Sunny has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sunny has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sunny showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Sunny traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Sunny entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Sunny paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Sunny story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 4 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who cannot win alone but somehow finds partners willing to carry the weight. But every season Sunny comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Sunny, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

Suraj has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Suraj paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In a sport where most kids quit after one bad tournament, Suraj has survived 2 seasons, and that alone is worth something. Not a trophy, obviously — Suraj knows that better than anyone — but something. We will be here next season, spreadsheet ready.

suraj_rajesh_kumar.name: Suraj Rajesh Kumar
suraj_rajesh_kumar.summary:
A California competitor, Suraj Rajesh Kumar is a U.S. Junior National Champion — crowned at the biggest event on the domestic calendar in BD U17. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 9 career victories across 31 tournaments, Suraj has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Suraj is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (5 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Suraj especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 5 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (12 entries) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U17) • 8 ORC titles.

Suraj has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 27 different players across the career:
  • Vaughn Zhao (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Suraj is still ascending.

Suraj has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Suraj has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Suraj has been seeded #1 or #2 in 7 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 9 titles, Suraj has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
suraj_rajesh_kumar.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Suraj Rajesh Kumar file just landed on our desk. Suraj hails from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. And yes, Suraj IS a Junior National Champion — BD U17 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 88-entry career across 31 tournaments with a 10% win rate, which means 79 times — 79! — Suraj went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 88 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Suraj saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 13th. The first title would not come until 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Suraj's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Suraj actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 11 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Can we talk about Suraj and Boys' Singles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 3 finals, 2 losses. Suraj can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Boys' Singles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Suraj still cannot reach it. Mixed Doubles finals and Suraj — name a more iconic tragedy. 3 trips to the championship match, and Suraj has turned 1 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Suraj's trophies to save time.

Here is a fun detail: in BS U19, Suraj used to be seeded #1 (at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC). The most recent seeding? #2 (at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC). Tournament committees are literally telling Suraj 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Suraj experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Suraj has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 Dave Freeman OLC, where Suraj entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Suraj does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Suraj has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 9 wins means Suraj is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Suraj plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 5 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Suraj can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 27 of them, to be precise. Suraj has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Suraj's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Charity Lam: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Charity Lam has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U15 — we hope Kayosh Kumar Mohapatra has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Suraj entered 12 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Suraj's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Suraj and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Suraj has been seeded in 16 events and underperformed the seeding 6 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Suraj takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U19, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Suraj. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Suraj of is playing it safe. Suraj has entered older age groups at 5 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Suraj has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Suraj has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Suraj is not just losing locally — Suraj is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kumar family travels cross-country so Suraj can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Suraj has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Suraj showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Suraj traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Suraj entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Suraj paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Suraj story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 9 titles across 6 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Singles. But every season Suraj comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Suraj, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.
