surya_peechara.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Surya Peechara has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Surya's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Surya is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Surya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Surya boasts a clutch 54% win rate in three-game matches (7/13), a biggest win margin of 37 points, 38 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively.

Surya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Yashas Cheerlavancha (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (10 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Erela Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Aditya Namboodiripad (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

Surya has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Surya has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Surya consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 3 titles, Surya has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
surya_peechara.roast:
Someone get the popcorn, because the Surya Peechara file just landed on our desk. Surya hails from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 56 career entries in 22 tournaments, Surya has 3 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 17 entries where Surya drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 56 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Surya saga began at 2023 South CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Surya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Surya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Surya is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Remember when Surya used to win BD U13? Because Surya sure does — probably replays it in slow motion every night before bed. Surya took gold at 2025 NE MassBad OLC, and since then? Best finish: 5th. The crown did not just slip — it packed its bags and left.

Can we talk about Surya and Mixed Doubles finals? Because this is starting to feel less like bad luck and more like a psychological condition. 2 finals, 2 losses. Surya can fight through every round, dismantle the bracket, arrive at the championship match in Mixed Doubles... and then something happens. The racket gets heavy. The legs turn to concrete. The shuttle starts moving in slow motion but somehow Surya still cannot reach it.

Surya's entire trophy case is Boys' Doubles, Boys' Doubles, and more Boys' Doubles. The other 36 entries in different disciplines have produced exactly zero hardware. Imagine being a restaurant that only serves one dish — except sometimes the dish is also mediocre.

Here is a fun detail: in BS U15, Surya used to be seeded #2 (at 2025 Fortius South CRC). The most recent seeding? #5 (at 2025 Capital NE OLC). Tournament committees are literally telling Surya 'you are getting worse at this' — in writing, on the official draw sheet, for everyone to see. That has to sting.

If Surya's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Surya has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 NE OLC (NVBC), where Surya entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Surya does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Surya has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 3 wins means Surya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Surya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Surya is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Surya and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Surya needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Surya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Surya's texts. The go-to partner is Yashas Cheerlavancha (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Arjun Rajkumar has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Surya's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Surya entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Surya's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Surya and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Surya has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

One thing nobody can accuse Surya of is playing it safe. Surya has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Surya has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Surya has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Surya is not just losing locally — Surya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Peechara family travels cross-country so Surya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Surya has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Surya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Surya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Surya entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Surya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Surya's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 3 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 53 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Surya is a player who former champion currently in witness protection from their own legacy. Will Surya figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Surya's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

surya_sivaprakash.name: Surya Sivaprakash
surya_sivaprakash.summary:
Surya Sivaprakash has stepped onto the competitive stage with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Surya's.

Surya competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
surya_sivaprakash.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Surya Sivaprakash, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Surya is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf), and so far, the only thing Surya has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Surya saga began at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Surya has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Surya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) in BS U11, which means Surya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Surya is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Surya wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now.

It all started at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Swarup has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Swarup saga began at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Swarup plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Swarup has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Swarup showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Swarup traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Swarup entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Swarup paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

With only 3 entries on the books, Swarup is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time. The circuit is long, the shuttles are expensive, and we are patient. Come back in a season, Swarup. We will have more material. We always do.

sydney_li.name: Sydney Li
sydney_li.summary:
Representing New Jersey, Sydney Li is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Sydney in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Sydney is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (14 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Sydney is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U15 GS, U17 GS, U19 GD, U15 GD) • Junior Nationals champion (GD U13) • 10 ORC titles.

With 26 different doubles partners over the years, Sydney adapts to anyone:
  • Jun Heng Lai (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Rocky Yang — 6 events, 1 title together in U19/XD, across 2 seasons
  • Micah Cruz — 2 events, 1 title together in GD/U19

A veteran of the junior circuit, Sydney has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Sydney has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Sydney has been seeded #1 or #2 in 9 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 13 titles, Sydney has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
sydney_li.roast:
Step right up and meet Sydney Li, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. And yes, Sydney IS a Junior National Champion — GD U13 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 77-entry career across 27 tournaments with a 17% win rate, which means 64 times — 64! — Sydney went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 77 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Sydney saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Sydney burst onto the scene with a title in GD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Sydney has delivered on some of those promises: 13 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Sydney promised and what Sydney has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 5 titles in 14 entries — a stretch where Sydney actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 6 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

In GD U19, Sydney was once THE player to beat — 1 title including at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19). These days, Sydney is finishing 2nd, which is the competitive equivalent of an ex showing up to your wedding with a better-looking date.

The seeding trajectory in GS U15 tells a story: #1 at 2022 NE CRC, down to #2 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. When the people whose job is to rank you start ranking you lower, that is not a slump — that is a trend line with a very clear direction, and it is not pointing up.

Now we arrive at the part of the program that Sydney's therapist probably knows by heart: the finals record. Brace yourself. Sydney has reached 12 championship matches across this career. That is 12 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 12 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19), where Sydney entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GD U17, XD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (8 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Sydney does own 13 actual titles, so it is not like Sydney has never won anything. But 12 finals losses versus 13 wins means Sydney is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sydney plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 5 doubles, 5 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sydney can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 26 of them, to be precise. Sydney has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Sydney's texts. The go-to partner is Samuel Jiaoyang Li (7 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 South ORC in GD U17 — we hope Yaorui Shan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Sydney entered 12 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Sydney's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Sydney and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Sydney has been seeded in 18 events and underperformed the seeding 6 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Sydney takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Sydney. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Sydney of is playing it safe. Sydney has entered older age groups at 14 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Sydney has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 12 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Sydney has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Sydney is not just losing locally — Sydney is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Sydney can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sydney has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sydney showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U17, which means Sydney traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

The Sydney story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 13 titles across 6 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who proof that glory has an expiration date and Sydney's is past due. But every season Sydney comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Sydney, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

sydney_stachovic.name: Sydney Stachovic
sydney_stachovic.summary:
Representing Illinois, Sydney Stachovic is a tenacious competitor with 3 quarterfinal-or-better finishes, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 6 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

A true triple-threat, Sydney competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Sydney has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Rishi Jagannath — a trusted XD partner (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Sydney has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Sydney brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
sydney_stachovic.roast:
Step right up and meet Sydney Stachovic, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Sydney's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Sydney keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Sydney saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sydney plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sydney has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sydney showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Sydney traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Sydney entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Sydney paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Sydney keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Sydney has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Sydney, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

sylvia_yuqing_mei.name: Sylvia Yuqing Mei
sylvia_yuqing_mei.summary:
Hailing from Washington, Sylvia Yuqing Mei has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Sylvia's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Sylvia has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Sylvia is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-3 finisher.

Sylvia has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Daniel Seungchan Park — 4 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Hansika - Malladi — 3 events in GD, best: #2

A veteran of the junior circuit, Sylvia has competed across U13, U15 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Over 4 seasons and 2 titles, Sylvia has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
sylvia_yuqing_mei.roast:
Allow us to introduce Sylvia Yuqing Mei, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 2 wins across 20 entries in 8 tournaments. That is a 10% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Sylvia shows up to a tournament, Sylvia pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Sylvia saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Sylvia burst onto the scene with a title in GS U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Sylvia has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Sylvia promised and what Sylvia has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Sylvia actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Sylvia is 0-for-1. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Sylvia plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Sylvia can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Sylvia has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Daniel Seungchan Park (4 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U15 — we hope Alex Anlai Song has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Sylvia's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Sylvia has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Sylvia has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Sylvia has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Sylvia showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U15, which means Sylvia traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Sylvia entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Sylvia paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Sylvia's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 2 titles shine bright, but they are surrounded by 18 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. Will Sylvia figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Sylvia's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

sze_kei_wong.name: Sze Kei Wong
sze_kei_wong.summary:
Out of New Jersey, Sze Kei Wong knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U13 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 6 entries across 2 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Sze has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Sze is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Sze has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Sriaa Senthilkumar (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Ethan Qiu (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Sze has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
sze_kei_wong.roast:
We regret to inform you that we have reviewed the complete tournament record of Sze Kei Wong, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 win across 6 entries in 2 tournaments. That is a 17% conversion rate, which means roughly 9 out of every 10 times Sze shows up to a tournament, Sze pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Sze and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Sze needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Sze has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 South OLC in XD U13, which means Sze traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

With only 6 entries on the books, Sze is basically a rookie — which is the nicest possible way of saying 'there is not enough data to roast properly yet.' But give it time.
